Text: We introduce an exponential random graph model for networks with a fixed degree distribution and with a tunable degree-degree correlation. It is found that negative correlation is irrelevant in that the percolation transition in the disassortative network belongs to the same universality class of the uncorrelated network. Positive correlation turns out to be relevant. The percolation transition in the assortative network is characterized by the non-diverging mean size of finite clusters and power-law scalings of the density of the largest cluster and the cluster size distribution in the non-percolating phase as well as at the critical point. Our results suggest that the unusual type percolation transition in the growing network models reported recently may be inherited from the assortative degree-degree correlation.

Text: We obtain new invariants of topological link concordance and homology cobordism of 3-manifolds from Hirzebruch-type intersection form defects of towers of iterated p-covers. Text: We experimentally investigate the temperature dependence of Rabi oscillations and Ramsey fringes in superconducting phase qubits driven by microwave pulses. Text: We reconstruct a rational Lax matrix of size R+1 from its spectral curve (the desingularization of the characteristic polynomial) and some additional data. All objects are described in the most explicit terms using Theta functions. Via a sequence of ``elementary twists'', we construct sequences of Lax matrices sharing the same spectral curve and polar structure and related by conjugations by rational matrices. Particular choices of elementary twists lead to construction of sequences of Lax matrices related to finite--band recurrence relations (i.e. difference operators) sharing the same shape. Recurrences of this kind are satisfied by several types of orthogonal and biorthogonal polynomials. Text: In this paper, the bit energy requirements of training-based transmission over block Rayleigh fading channels are studied. Pilot signals are employed to obtain the minimum mean-square-error (MMSE) estimate of the channel fading coefficients. Energy efficiency is analyzed in the worst case scenario where the channel estimate is assumed to be perfect and the error in the estimate is considered as another source of additive Gaussian noise. It is shown that bit energy requirement grows without bound as the snr goes to zero, and the minimum bit energy is achieved at a nonzero snr value below which one should not operate. The effect of the block length on both the minimum bit energy and the snr value at which the minimum is achieved is investigated. Flash training schemes are analyzed and shown to improve the energy efficiency in the low-snr regime. Energy efficiency analysis is also carried out when peak power constraints are imposed on pilot signals.

Text: A Brownian Motor is a nanoscale or molecular device that combines the effects of thermal noise, spatial or temporal asymmetry, and directionless input energy to drive directed motion. Because of the input energy, Brownian motors function away from thermodynamic equilibrium and concepts such as linear response theory, fluctuation dissipation relations, and detailed balance do not apply. The {\em generalized} fluctuation-dissipation relation, however, states that even under strongly thermodynamically non-equilibrium conditions the ratio of the probability of a transition to the probability of the time-reverse of that transition is the exponent of the change in the internal energy of the system due to the transition. Here, we derive an extension of the generalized fluctuation dissipation theorem for a Brownian motor for the ratio between the probability for the motor to take a forward step and the probability to take a backward step.

It also allows a quantitative assessment of the errors and enables a rigorous testing of the significance of relationships between various curve parameters, regardless of whether their uncertainties are correlated. Analysis of the curves gives the following results: (1) In accord with our previous findings, the central position of the 2175 A extinction bump is mildly variable, its width is highly variable, and the two variations are unrelated. (2) Strong correlations are found among some extinction properties within the UV region, and within the IR region. (3) With the exception of a few curves with extreme (i.e., large) values of R(V), the UV and IR portions of Galactic extinction curves are not correlated with each other. (4)

Text: The fractional quantum Hall effect (FQHE) has been a topic of intense research in condensed matter physics due to its exotic properties and potential applications in quantum computing. Our results show that the noise spectrum exhibits distinct peaks at certain frequencies, which can be attributed to the emission and absorption of quasiparticles in the edge states. Our findings shed light on the fundamental mechanism of noise generation in the FQHE system and have implications for the design of FQHE-based quantum devices.

Text: The stability of large-scale magnetic fields in rotating stars is explored, using 3D numerical hydrodynamics to follow the evolution of an initial poloidal field. It is found that the field is subject to an instability, located initially on the magnetic equator, whereby the gas is displaced in a direction parallel to the magnetic axis. If the magnetic axis is parallel to the rotation axis, the rotation does not affect the initial linear growth of the instability, but does restrict the growth of the instability outside of the equatorial zone. The magnetic energy decays on a timescale which is a function of the Alfv\'en crossing time and the rotation speed, but short compared to any evolutionary timescale. No evidence is found for a possible stable end state to evolve from an initial axisymmetric poloidal field. The field of an oblique rotator is similarly unstable, in both cases regardless of the rotation speed.

Text: We show that a quantum state may be represented as the sum of a joint probability and a complex quantum modification term. A selective phase rotation is needed to obtain the imaginary part. This leads to a complex quasiprobability, the Kirkwood distribution. We show that the Kirkwood distribution contains full information about the state if the two observables are maximal and complementary. The Kirkwood distribution gives a new picture of state reduction. In a nonselective measurement, the modification term vanishes. A selective measurement leads to a quantum state as a nonnegative conditional probability. We demonstrate the special significance of the Schwinger basis.

Text: Evolutionary Learning proceeds by evolving a population of classifiers, from which it generally returns (with some notable exceptions) the single best-of-run classifier as final result. In the meanwhile, Ensemble Learning, one of the most efficient approaches in supervised Machine Learning for the last decade, proceeds by building a population of diverse classifiers. Ensemble Learning with Evolutionary Computation thus receives increasing attention. The Evolutionary Ensemble Learning (EEL) approach presented in this paper features two contributions. First, a new fitness function, inspired by co-evolution and enforcing the classifier diversity, is presented. Further, a new selection criterion based on the classification margin is proposed. This criterion is used to extract the classifier ensemble from the final population only (Off-line) or incrementally along evolution (On-line). Experiments on a set of benchmark problems show that Off-line outperforms single-hypothesis evolutionary learning and state-of-art Boosting and generates smaller classifier ensembles.

Text: The holographic principle states that on a fundamental level the information content of a region should depend on its surface area rather than on its volume. This counterintuitive idea which has its roots in the nonextensive nature of black-hole entropy serves as a guiding principle in the search for the fundamental laws of Planck-scale physics. In this paper we show that a similar phenomenon emerges from the established laws of classical and quantum physics: the information contained in part of a system in thermal equilibrium obeys an area law. While the maximal information per unit area depends classically only on the number of microscopic degrees of freedom, it may diverge as the inverse temperature in quantum systems. A rigorous relation between area laws and correlations is established and their explicit behavior is revealed for a large class of quantum many-body states beyond equilibrium systems.

Text: A recent, major, puzzle in the core-level photoemission spectra of doped manganites is the observation of a 1-2 eV wide shoulder with intensity varying with temperature T as the square of the magnetization over a T scale of order 200K, an order of magnitude less than electronic energies. This is addressed and resolved here, by extending a recently proposed two electron fluid l-b model for these systems to include core-hole effects. The shoulder arises from a rapid redistribution of e_g electron density, as a function of T, between the highly localized (l) and band-like (b) states. Furthermore, our theory leads to a correspondence between spectral changes due to increasing doping and decreasing T, as experimentally observed.

Text: We present a rigorous calculation of the dynamical friction force exerted on a spherical massive perturber moving through an infinite homogenous system of field stars. By calculating the shape and mass of the polarization cloud induced by the perturber in the background system, which decelerates the motion of the perturber, we recover Chandrasekhar's drag force law with a modified Coulomb logarithm. As concrete examples we calculate the drag force exerted on a Plummer sphere or a sphere with the density distribution of a Hernquist profile. It is shown that the shape of the perturber affects only the exact form of the Coulomb logarithm. The latter converges on small scales, because encounters of the test and field stars with impact parameters less than the size of the massive perturber become inefficient. We confirm this way earlier results based on the impulse approximation of small angle scatterings.

Text: In the last few decades, some hypotheses for entropy production (EP) principles have been forwarded as possible candidates for organizational principles in non-linear non- equilibrium systems. Two important hypotheses will be studied: the maximum entropy production (MaxEP) principle that claims that the selected steady state has the highest EP, and the gradient response principle that claims that the EP of the selected steady state (maximally) increases when the external thermodynamic driving force increases. We will formulate these hypotheses more rigorously and present a simple chemical reaction model to test these hypotheses. With the help of this model, we will clearly demonstrate that there are different MaxEP hypotheses being discussed in the literature and we will look at some parts in the literature where these differences are not always clarified. Furthermore, our chemical model will be a general counter example to all of these MaxEP and gradient response hypotheses.

Text: Interaction between metal surface waves and periodic geometry of subwavelength structures is at the core of the recent but crucial renewal of interest in plasmonics. The actual paradigm is that this abnormal transmission arises from the periodicity, and then that such high transmission should disappear in random geometries. Here, we show that extra energy can be coupled through the subwavelength structure by adding a controlled quantity of noise to the position of the apertures. This result can be modelled in the statistical framework of stochastic resonance. The evolution of the coupled energy with respect to noise gives access to the extra energy coupled at the surface of the subwavelength array.

Text: Efficient injection of spin-polarized electrons into the conduction band of silicon is limited by the formation of a silicide at the ferromagnetic metal (FM)/silicon interface. In the present work, this "magnetically-dead" silicide (where strong spin-scattering significantly reduces injected spin polarization) is eliminated by moving the FM in the spin injector from the tunnel junction base anode to the emitter cathode and away from the silicon surface. This results in over an order-of-magnitude increase in spin injection efficiency, from a previously-reported magnetocurrent ratio of ~2% to ~35% and an estimated spin polarization in Si from ~1% to at least ~15%. The injector tunnel-junction bias dependence of this spin transport signal is also measured, demonstrating the importance of low bias voltage to preserve high injected spin polarization.

Here, we present results on local electromechanical probing of several model cellular and biomolecular systems, including insulin and lysozyme amyloid fibrils, breast adenocarcinoma cells, and bacteriorhodopsin in a liquid environment. Text: We give a one-dimensional quantum cellular automaton (QCA) capable of simulating all others. By this we mean that the initial configuration and the local transition rule of any one-dimensional QCA can be encoded within the initial configuration of the universal QCA. Several steps of the universal QCA will then correspond to one step of the simulated QCA. The simulation preserves the topology in the sense that each cell of the simulated QCA is encoded as a group of adjacent cells in the universal QCA. The encoding is linear and hence does not carry any of the cost of the computation. We do this in two flavours: a weak one which requires an infinite but periodic initial configuration and a strong one which needs only a finite initial configuration. KEYWORDS: Quantum cellular automata, Intrinsic universality, Quantum computation.

Tensor equations in this setting, also called functional tensor equations, describe families of functional equations on various Hilbert spaces of functions. The principle of functional relativity is introduced which states that quantum theory is indeed a functional tensor theory, i.e., it can be described by functional tensor equations. The main equations of quantum theory are shown to be compatible with the principle of functional relativity. By accepting the principle as a hypothesis, we then analyze the origin of physical dimensions, provide a geometric interpretation of Planck's constant, and find a simple interpretation of the two-slit experiment and the process of measurement.

Text: Hypermultiplet couplings in type IIA string theory on a Calabi-Yau space can be quantum corrected by D2-brane instantons wrapping special Lagrangian cycles. On the other hand, hypermultiplet couplings in the heterotic string on a K3 surface are corrected by world-sheet instantons wrapping curves. In a class of examples, we relate these two sets of instanton corrections. We first present an analogue of the c-map for the heterotic string via a dual flux compactification of M-theory. Using this duality, we propose two ways of capturing quantum corrections to hypermultiplets. We then use the orientifold limit of certain F-theory compactifications to relate curves in K3 to special Lagrangians in dual type IIA compactifications. We conclude with some results from perturbative string theory for hypermultiplet F-terms and a conjecture about the topology of brane instantons.

Text: We analyze a large-scale snapshot of del.icio.us and investigate how the number of different tags in the system grows as a function of a suitably defined notion of time. Surprisingly, the observed growth behaviors are remarkably regular throughout the entire history of the system and across very different resources being bookmarked. Similar sub-linear laws of growth have been observed in written text, and this qualitative universality calls for an explanation and points in the direction of non-trivial cognitive processes in the complex interaction patterns characterizing collaborative tagging.

The nonlinear Schr\"odinger (NLS) equation that one can derive from the new set of equations to describe the modulations of weakly nonlinear, weakly damped deep-water gravity waves turns out to be the classical damped version of the NLS equation that has been used by many authors without rigorous justification.

Text: We consider a family of models describing the evolution under selection of a population whose dynamics can be related to the propagation of noisy traveling waves. We also find that in the exponential model, the coalescence times along these trees grow like the logarithm of the population size. Text: We study to what extent wormholes can mimic the observational features of black holes. It is surprisingly found that many features that could be thought of as ``characteristic'' of a black hole (endowed with an event horizon) can be closely mimicked by a globally static wormhole, having no event horizon. This is the case for: the apparently irreversible accretion of matter down a hole, no-hair properties, quasi-normal-mode ringing, and even the dissipative properties of black hole horizons, such as a finite surface resistivity equal to 377 Ohms. The only way to distinguish the two geometries on an observationally reasonable time scale would be through the detection of Hawking's radiation, which is, however, too weak to be of practical relevance for astrophysical black holes. We point out the existence of an interesting spectrum of quantum microstates trapped in the throat of a wormhole which could be relevant for storing the information ``lost'' during a gravitational collapse.

The basic modules of our lung-CAD system, a dot-enhancement filter for nodule candidate selection and a neural classifier for false-positive finding reduction, are described. The system was designed and tested for both internal and sub-pleural nodules. The results obtained on the collected database of low-dose thin-slice CT scans are shown in terms of free response receiver operating characteristic (FROC) curves and discussed.

Text: The diagram algebra introduced by Brauer that describes the centralizer algebra on tensor products of the natural representation of an orthogonal group has a presentation by generators and relations that only depends on the graph of type An on n nodes. Here we describe an algebra depending on an arbitrary graph of type M. We study its structure when the type is An, Dn, E6, E7, E8. We determine the representations and find the dimensions. The algebra is generically semisimple and contains the group algebra of the Coxeter type M as a subalgebra. It is a ring homomorphism of the Birman-Murakami-Wenzl algebra of these types. This fact will be used in later work determining the structure of Birman-Murakami-Wenzl algebras of simply laced spherical type.

Text: We study the effects of charge polarization on the extended physical properties of superlattices, such as transmission coefficients and valence band structure. Text: Interrelation between the two-particle and mean-field problems is used to describe the strong pseudogap and superconducting states in cuprates. A competition between the ODSRO and ODLRO states leads to the phase diagram typical of the cuprates. Text: This work considers the problem of transmitting multiple compressible sources over a network at minimum cost. The problem is complicated by the fact that the description of the feasible rate region of distributed source coding problems typically has a number of constraints that is exponential in the number of sources. This renders general purpose solvers inefficient. We present a framework in which these problems can be solved efficiently by exploiting the structure of the feasible rate regions coupled with dual decomposition and optimization techniques such as the subgradient method and the proximal bundle method.

Text: In the theory of operads we consider functors of generalized symmetric powers defined by sums of coinvariant modules under actions of symmetric groups. One observes classically that the construction of symmetric functors provides an isomorphism from the category of symmetric modules to a subcategory of the category of functors on the base category. The purpose of this book is to obtain a similar relationship for functors on a category of algebras over an operad. We observe that right modules over operads, symmetric modules equipped with a right operad action, give rise to functors on categories of algebras and we prove that this construction yields an embedding of categories. Then we check that right modules over operads form a model category. In addition we prove that weak-equivalences of right modules correspond to pointwise weak-equivalences at the functor level. As a conclusion, we obtain that right modules over operads supply good models for the homotopy of associated functors on algebras over operads.

Text: The thermodynamics of black holes is reformulated within the context of the recently developed formalism of geometrothermodynamics. On the contrary, for the Kerr black hole the geometry is flat and does not explain its phase transition structure.

Text: We show that the van der Waals-like phase separation instability of a driven granular gas at zero gravity, previously investigated in two-dimensional settings, persists in three dimensions. When the inelastic energy loss is sufficiently high, the driven granular gas exhibits, in some range of average densities, negative compressibility in the directions parallel to the driving wall. Text: Many important real-world networks manifest "small-world" properties such as scale-free degree distributions, small diameters, and clustering. We present a new single-parameter model of network growth that unifies varieties of preferential attachment with the quasispecies equation (which models molecular evolution), and also with the Erdos-Renyi random graph model. We suggest some properties of evolutionary models that might be applied to the study of networks. We also derive the form of the degree distribution resulting from our algorithm, and we show through simulations that the process also models aspects of network growth. The unification allows mathematical machinery developed for evolutionary dynamics to be applied in the study of network dynamics, and vice versa.

Text: We present a quantum auction protocol using superpositions to represent bids and distributed search to identify the winner(s). Text: The Positive Mass Conjecture states that any complete asymptotically flat manifold of nonnnegative scalar curvature has nonnegative mass. We show that, whenever the Positive Mass Theorem holds, any appropriately normalized sequence of such manifolds whose masses converge to zero must have metrics that are uniformly converging to Euclidean metrics outside a compact region. Text: The security of lattice-based cryptosystems such as NTRU, GGH and Ajtai-Dwork essentially relies upon the intractability of computing a shortest non-zero lattice vector and a closest lattice vector to a given target vector in high dimensions. The best algorithms for these tasks are due to Kannan, and, though remarkably simple, their complexity estimates have not been improved since more than twenty years. Kannan's algorithm for solving the shortest vector problem is in particular crucial in Schnorr's celebrated block reduction algorithm, on which are based the best known attacks against the lattice-based encryption schemes mentioned above. Understanding precisely Kannan's algorithm is of prime importance for providing meaningful key-sizes. In this paper we improve the complexity analyses of Kannan's algorithms and discuss the possibility of improving the underlying enumeration strategy.

By coupling them to an electromagnetic field and expanding the square root (and taking into account terms of quadratic order in the electromagnetic coupling constant e), it is possible to calculate scattering matrix elements within the framework of quantum electrodynamics, e.g. like those for Compton scattering or for the scattering of two identical particles. Text: Many extra-solar planets discovered over the past decade are gas giants in tight orbits around their host stars. Due to the difficulties of forming these `hot Jupiters' in situ, they are generally assumed to have migrated to their present orbits through interactions with their nascent discs. In this paper, we present a systematic study of giant planet migration in power law discs. We find that the planetary migration rate is proportional to the disc surface density. This is inconsistent with the assumption that the migration rate is simply the viscous drift speed of the disc. However, this result can be obtained by balancing the angular momentum of the planet with the viscous torque in the disc. We have verified that this result is not affected by adjusting the resolution of the grid, the smoothing length used, or the time at which the planet is released to migrate.

Text: In the presence of Byzantine attacks on sensors or encoders, the distributed source coding problem becomes increasingly complex. This is characterized by the maximum entropy of distributions indistinguishable from the true source distribution. First, resonant transport through an embedded quantum dot provides a means to extract \( K \) via conductance scaling. Second, the charge on the dot, analyzed through power-law fluctuations, offers an independent determination of \( K \). Agreement between these two measurements for a single system would constitute robust evidence for Luttinger liquid theory, reinforcing its predictive framework in low-dimensional electron systems. This method eliminates ambiguity by cross-verifying \( K \) through distinct experimental probes, ensuring consistency with theoretical expectations.

Text: The study highlights a significant detection rate (30%) of H₂ jets associated with sources embedded within millimeter-wavelength cores. However, the statistical assessment of core activity versus inactivity, while consistent with observations in other star-forming regions, may be influenced by the sensitivity limitations of the observational instruments rather than intrinsic properties of the millimeter condensations. Text: A lattice-based model is introduced to study particle diffusion within the cell nucleus, where chromatin fibers form dense networks. The study systematically explores how 3D network geometry—including occupation volume, chain length, persistence length, and particle size—influences diffusional transport. Key findings indicate that monomer adjacency, excluded volume effects in self-avoiding walks, and, to a lesser extent, persistence length, modulate diffusion. The introduction of effective chain occupancy, a composite measure of geometric chain volume and particle size, resolves conformational dependencies, providing a refined framework for understanding nuclear transport dynamics.

Text: A discrete tensegrity framework may be modeled as a graph embedded in Euclidean *n*-space, where edges are categorized into three types: fixed-length bars, cables with upper length bounds, and struts with lower length bounds. This equivalence holds if a positive stress vector exists, assigning weights to edges such that the weighted sum of edge vectors vanishes at every vertex. This study extends the framework by considering a generalized configuration where vertices form an arbitrary (possibly infinite) set of points in Euclidean *n*-space, and edges constitute a compact collection of unordered pairs. The analysis retains the foundational principles of tensegrity while accommodating broader geometric constraints.

Text: This study resolves a long-standing question in algorithmic game theory by establishing that the Bimatrix problem—finding a Nash equilibrium in two-player games—is PPAD-complete. This classification, introduced by Papadimitriou (1991), confirms that Bimatrix is representative of the computational challenges inherent in the PPAD class. Key findings include: (1) Bimatrix lacks a fully polynomial-time approximation scheme unless PPAD is solvable in polynomial time; (2) the Lemke-Howson algorithm and any Bimatrix algorithm exhibit non-polynomial smoothed complexity unless PPAD is tractable in randomized polynomial time. These results underscore the equivalence between equilibrium computation and fixed-point problems in non-cooperative games. Additionally, they extend to broader implications in mathematical economics and operations research, demonstrating that Arrow-Debreu market equilibria and P-Matrix linear complementarity problems are PPAD-hard. The work thus provides a foundational framework for understanding the computational limits of equilibrium-based models.

Text: The stability of large-scale magnetic fields in rotating stars is examined through 3D hydrodynamic simulations, tracking the evolution of an initial poloidal field. Text: Evolutionary Learning typically evolves a population of classifiers, ultimately selecting the single best-performing individual as the final output. This approach introduces two key innovations: a novel fitness function, derived from co-evolution principles, that promotes classifier diversity, and a selection criterion based on classification margin. This criterion enables either offline selection from the final population or online extraction during evolution. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the offline method surpasses traditional single-hypothesis evolutionary learning and state-of-the-art boosting techniques while producing smaller, more efficient ensembles.

Simulations suggest that upcoming low-redshift supernova surveys will achieve a 5% precision in constraining the matter fluctuation amplitude (σ₈) at 95% confidence, rivaling the precision expected from future galaxy and weak lensing surveys. Text: Fractional calculus extends the diffusion equation by substituting either the first-order time derivative or the second-order spatial derivative with a fractional-order derivative. The emergence of stable distributions in fractional diffusion models is particularly significant in financial and economic contexts, where such distributions are pivotal for modeling asset prices, risk, and other stochastic processes. The flexibility of fractional derivatives allows for more accurate representations of phenomena exhibiting non-Gaussian behavior, enhancing the applicability of diffusion-based models in complex systems.

Text: Quantum entanglement describes a state in which a composite system exhibits well-defined properties, while its subsystems remain undetermined until measurement. This phenomenon is fundamental to quantum mechanics and plays a crucial role in quantum information science. A notable feature of entanglement is its ability to be "swapped": by performing a Bell-state measurement (BSM) on two independent entangled pairs (A1-A2 and B1-B2), the previously uncorrelated subsystems A2 and B2 become entangled, despite lacking direct interaction. Prior demonstrations of entanglement swapping relied on twin photons generated via spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC). However, this work presents the first experimental realization using continuous-wave (CW) sources, as initially proposed. The key challenge involved achieving precise synchronization of photons in the BSM, addressed through the use of narrow-band filters to extend coherence time.

Text: The power-law fits of the cumulative phase distribution (CPD) for giant pulses (GPs) exhibit breaks, indicating a flattening at lower energies. These observations are compared with existing literature to contextualize their statistical significance. The distinct characteristics of GPs—such as their energy-dependent spectral behavior and duration-flux density relationship—provide critical insights into the underlying emission mechanisms. Understanding these properties is essential for refining theoretical models of GP generation, as they reveal key aspects of the radiative processes involved. The findings contribute to a broader framework for interpreting the statistical and physical nature of these extreme astrophysical phenomena.

Text: Disclinations, initially identified in mesomorphic phases, are significant in various disordered condensed matter systems exhibiting continuous symmetries or frustrated order. The dynamics of disclinations—including their formation, motion, and deformation—involve interactions with continuous or quantized dislocations and disclinations, either attached to them or described by Nye’s dislocation densities. These processes are encompassed by the "extended Volterra process," which accounts for such interactions in diverse scenarios. Applications in amorphous solids, mesomorphic phases, and frustrated media within curved configurations exemplify these principles. Topological theories of line defects focus solely on defects stable against relaxation mechanisms consistent with the system’s structure.

Text: A coordinate-invariant description of local geometry in three-dimensional gravity is achieved through a novel adaptation of Karlhede’s invariant classification. As an application, the study examines Gödel-type spacetimes in three-dimensional General Relativity, deriving conditions for local homogeneity in space and time. The equivalence of these spacetimes is investigated, with results compared to prior work on four-dimensional Gödel-type geometries. Notably, the violation of BCHSH inequalities persists even with an arbitrarily large number of spins, potentially matching or exceeding the violation observed with just two spins. This phenomenon highlights the interplay between quantum coherence and measurement-induced classicality in many-body systems.

Text: A real-space quantum transport simulator has been employed to analyze the performance of carbon nanoribbon (CNR) MOSFETs under ballistic transport conditions. In contrast, pure 1D devices with infinite contacts exhibit no MIGS, though quantum tunneling remains significant. Band-to-band tunneling, accurately modeled in the simulations, dominates the OFF-state current. Text: Heterogeneous phase response curves (PRCs) are prevalent in real-world oscillator systems, yet their impact on network dynamics remains understudied. Text: The conformational dynamics of large DNA molecules in bulk solution, influenced by condensing agents, have been examined through experimental and theoretical methods. The simulations demonstrate that hysteresis arises in sufficiently rigid chains, indicating distinct kinetic pathways for folding and unfolding. Text: The relaxation dynamics of a colloidal fluid composed of platelike particles is analyzed using a phenomenological dynamic density functional theory. The system is initially a spatially homogeneous isotropic fluid, either stable or metastable, perturbed by activating a two-dimensional array of Gaussian laser beams. This induces particle accumulation at beam centers. For sufficiently high initial chemical potential and laser power, particles develop a preferred orientation, breaking the symmetry of the laser potential. Text: Hydrodynamic systems exhibiting a local Hamiltonian structure of Dubrovin-Novikov type inherently possess an infinite family of nonlocal Hamiltonian structures. Furthermore, the results are applied to reciprocal systems derived from genus *g* Whitham-KdV modulation equations, illustrating the broader applicability of the theoretical framework. The findings contribute to the understanding of structural invariance in Hamiltonian hydrodynamic systems under reciprocal transformations.

Text: In two-way wire-tap channels, two legitimate users exchange information while an eavesdropper intercepts transmissions via a multiple-access channel. The analysis reveals that two-way communication inherently enhances security, as users leverage their knowledge of their own transmitted signals to effectively encrypt the other party's messages, analogous to a one-time pad. Comparisons with the Gaussian multiple-access wire-tap channel (GMAC-WT) demonstrate the superior secrecy performance of two-way channels, highlighting their potential for secure communication in adversarial environments. The results underscore the cryptographic benefits of bidirectional transmission in combating eavesdropping.

Text: To enhance the accuracy of celestial object identification, radio and optical images must be correlated. This study examines the influence of galactic radio refraction, which alters the apparent coordinates of radio sources relative to their optical counterparts. By accounting for this refraction, the majority of radio sources can be accurately matched with optical objects, improving cross-wavelength identification. This correction mitigates prior limitations, ensuring more reliable astronomical data integration across different observational bands.

Text: The transverse dynamics of stiff polymers, modeled as weakly bending wormlike chains (WLCs), are well-characterized under linear conditions, where transverse and longitudinal degrees of freedom decouple. However, nonlinear coupling between these modes becomes significant beyond a critical timescale in inextensible WLCs, substantially reducing the polymer's response compared to linear predictions. This feedback effect is explained through scaling arguments and quantified using a multiscale analysis, leveraging the distinct transverse and longitudinal correlation lengths. Derived scaling laws and exact solutions—both analytical and numerical—describe key response metrics for various experimental configurations. These insights are relevant to cytoskeletal filaments and DNA under tension, offering a refined understanding of nonlinear polymer dynamics.  (Word count: 120)

Text: Early-R stars are carbon-rich K-type giants exhibiting elevated levels of carbon-12, carbon-13, and nitrogen-14, while maintaining near-solar oxygen, magnesium isotope ratios, s-process element abundances, and iron content. Their peculiar nature defies conventional single-star evolutionary models, prompting the hypothesis that they originate from binary star mergers. Through population synthesis of binary systems, we identified multiple potential evolutionary pathways, with the most prevalent involving the merger of a helium white dwarf with a hydrogen-burning red giant branch star during a common envelope phase, followed by a helium flash that induces carbon dredge-up. Collectively, these channels overproduce early-R stars by an order of magnitude compared to Hipparcos observations, suggesting the need for refined theoretical constraints.

Applying this approach within the self-consistent Born approximation—ensuring energy conservation—the investigation examines heat dissipation in current-carrying atomic junctions. The findings demonstrate that phonon transport significantly influences both heat dissipation and temperature dynamics in such systems, underscoring its critical role in nanoscale thermal management.

Text: This study examines the mixing time of a systematic scan Markov chain designed to sample uniformly from proper 7-colorings of finite rectangular sub-grids of the infinite square lattice. A computer-assisted proof demonstrates that this chain achieves mixing in O(log n) scans, where n denotes the grid size. The analysis relies on a heuristic to compute necessary couplings of 2×2 sub-grid colorings. Notably, this represents the first rigorous demonstration of sub-exponential mixing for a systematic scan Markov chain on the grid with fewer than 8 colors. Text: Microwave phonon stimulated emission (SE) has been extensively studied both experimentally and numerically in nonautonomous microwave acoustic quantum generators, commonly referred to as microwave phonon lasers or phasers. As pumping intensity increases, the number of coexisting SE states grows, as observed in both physical experiments and computational simulations, aligning with theoretical predictions of branching density in phase space. The refractority of SE pulses is intrinsically linked to this branching behavior, reflecting crises in strange attractors, particularly their collisions with unstable periodic components of higher-order SE states within the nonautonomous phonon laser framework.

Text: This study examines the mathematical modeling of nuclear waste contamination, incorporating thermal effects. A hybrid numerical approach combining finite volume and nonconforming finite element methods is employed to discretize the problem. The proposed scheme is rigorously analyzed, demonstrating stability and convergence properties, along with associated error estimates. This framework provides a robust computational tool for simulating contaminant transport in porous media under varying thermal conditions.

Text: We investigate the ground-state energies of a heavy-light K-Λ-like system as a function of the inter-hadron distance \( r \). The study employs an anisotropic and asymmetric lattice with Wilson fermions, and energies are derived from spectral density functions via the maximum entropy method. The results provide qualitative insights: solving the Schrödinger equation with the adiabatic potential yields bound-state wave functions suggesting the ground state resembles a hadronic molecule, while the first excited state, with a compact radius, is more consistent with a five-quark cluster or pentaquark. We propose that all-light-quark pentaquarks may not exist, but heavy-quark pentaquarks could, though likely only in excited states.

Text: Versatile parametrization framework for dark energy models, encompassing modified gravity theories and extended dark energy formulations. Additionally, such surveys can constrain the perturbation growth index with high precision, achieving absolute errors of approximately 0.02–0.03. These results underscore the diagnostic power of weak lensing observations in probing fundamental aspects of cosmic acceleration and gravitational physics.

Simulations across a broad range of initial conditions—parameterized by a single dimensionless variable—show excellent agreement with exact Vlasov-Poisson theory, offering a comprehensive characterization of all expansion stages. Text: Mammoths inhabited Arctic East Siberia, where insufficient annual sunlight prevented the growth of their primary plant-based food sources. This suggests that the region’s latitude was lower before the Pleistocene’s conclusion. A hypothesized massive object, orbiting eccentrically and heated by tidal forces and solar radiation, may have caused this geographic shift. The object’s evaporation generated a disk-shaped ion cloud around the Sun, partially blocking solar radiation and inducing Pleistocene-era climate fluctuations. These fluctuations, tied to Earth’s orbital inclination (with a ~100,000-year cycle), resulted in periodic warming and cooling events. The ion cloud’s density eventually triggered inelastic collisions, leading to collapse and cyclical climate patterns resembling Dansgaard-Oeschger events. Fine-grained particles were deposited in ice during colder phases. The Pleistocene epoch terminated when the massive object passed close to Earth, disrupting the system.

This work underscores the importance of solution regularity in accurately modeling material behavior under softening conditions.

Text: In operad theory, functors of generalized symmetric powers are defined via sums of coinvariant modules under symmetric group actions. Furthermore, we establish that right operad modules form a model category, where weak equivalences correspond to pointwise weak equivalences at the functor level. Consequently, right operad modules provide robust models for studying the homotopy of associated functors on algebras over operads.

Text: Non-Abelian discrete symmetries have been increasingly employed to elucidate the flavor structure of leptons and quarks. Conversely, other models posit that these particles reside within the TeV range, thereby enhancing the likelihood of their observation at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at CERN. Text: In wireless packet-forwarding networks with selfish nodes, repeated game strategies can incentivize cooperation by encouraging mutual packet forwarding, thereby enhancing overall network performance. To address this, coalition game theory is employed, enabling boundary nodes to engage in cooperative transmission with backbone nodes. In exchange, backbone nodes reciprocate by forwarding boundary nodes' packets. The stability of these coalitions is analyzed using core theory, while fairness is evaluated through min-max fairness (via nucleolus) and average fairness (via Shapley value). A hybrid protocol integrating repeated and coalition games is then developed. Simulation results demonstrate the protocol's effectiveness in mitigating the boundary node issue, improving network efficiency and fairness.

Notably, despite its high mobility, the quantum Hall effect is absent in epitaxial graphene, likely due to the lack of localized bulk states. Text: A phenomenological study of diffractive hadroproduction of heavy quarks (charm, bottom, and top) at high energies is presented. Projections for future measurements at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) are also offered, facilitating comparisons with anticipated high-energy collider results. This work advances the understanding of diffractive heavy-flavor dynamics in high-energy hadronic interactions.

Text: Exoplanets transiting their host stars—those with orbital inclinations near 90 degrees—offer unique opportunities to study their physical properties. By analyzing the host star’s parameters, the planetary radius can be precisely determined. When combined with spectroscopic Doppler measurements, the planet’s mass and density can be derived. Additionally, if the host star is sufficiently bright, further insights, such as the spin-orbit alignment between the star and planetary orbit, may be obtained.  Recent advancements in transit surveys, including contributions from the MACHO project, have identified multiple potential transiting exoplanets, some orbiting relatively bright stars. However, photometric transit data alone cannot definitively confirm the planetary nature of the companion. This study therefore focuses on determining the spectroscopic parameters of the host stars and conducting dynamical analyses to verify the planetary candidates.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines the additivity properties of classical capacity, minimal output entropy, and its convex closure in quantum channels. We demonstrate that additivity for arbitrary channel pairs holds if and only if it holds for equal pairs, which may be further restricted to unital channels. Additionally, we establish that weak additivity implies strong additivity for any convex entanglement monotone. These results are derived by analyzing direct sums of channels or states, allowing information-theoretic quantities to be expressed in terms of their summands. This approach provides a versatile method for extending additivity results, offering a unified framework for such analyses. The findings enhance the understanding of quantum information theory by clarifying the conditions under which additivity holds and its implications for entanglement measures.

Text: The study examines pattern formation and evolution in complex networks that resist complete synchronization due to asymmetric structural properties. Near the boundary of synchronizability, the system exhibits on-off intermittency, where dynamics alternate between synchronized and desynchronized states. These patterns feature a dominant "giant cluster" encompassing most nodes alongside smaller clusters. The giant cluster dynamically absorbs or releases smaller clusters in an irregular manner. As coupling strength diverges further from the synchronization threshold, the giant cluster disintegrates into smaller clusters, with system dynamics governed by their repeated fusion and fragmentation. Investigations reveal underlying mechanisms and statistical properties, including novel scaling laws. Notably, a small subset of active nodes consistently evades synchronization, influencing overall network behavior. These findings enhance understanding of nonstationary pattern dynamics in complex systems.

Text: The classical definition of \wp, traditionally used to describe far-field phenomena, has been widely applied to various \w processes over the past decades. To address these challenges, a novel local approach for defining \wvs is introduced, which resolves these inconsistencies while retaining classical definitions as special cases. This generalized method extends beyond conventional non-local models and can be applied to diverse \w forms and propagation media. The formalism’s key applications and fundamental properties are outlined, demonstrating its broader utility in wave physics. The proposed framework provides a more robust and versatile characterization of \w phenomena, accommodating both classical and anomalous behaviors.

Text: Employing the previously established confining gluon propagator, the governing equations for the quark propagator are systematically derived. This framework comprises the Schwinger-Dyson equation for the quark propagator, incorporating the zero-momentum quark-gluon vertex, alongside the Slavnov-Taylor identity for this vertex. The quark propagator equation explicitly incorporates the mass gap, a critical parameter defining the scale of nonperturbative QCD dynamics in the vacuum state. The resulting system of equations exhibits manifest gauge invariance, as it remains independent of the gauge-fixing parameter. Additionally, it is devoid of perturbative artifacts that might otherwise arise at the fundamental quark-gluon interaction level. This ensures a purely nonperturbative description of quark dynamics within the QCD framework.

Text: Spin injection efficiency into silicon is hindered by the formation of a magnetically inactive silicide layer at the ferromagnetic metal/silicon interface, which induces strong spin scattering and reduces polarization. By relocating the ferromagnetic material from the tunnel junction anode to the emitter cathode, this detrimental silicide layer is avoided, enhancing spin injection efficiency by over an order of magnitude. The magnetocurrent ratio improves from ~2% to ~35%, while spin polarization in silicon increases from ~1% to at least ~15%. Additionally, the bias dependence of the spin transport signal is analyzed, revealing that low bias voltages are critical for maintaining high spin polarization levels. These findings underscore the significance of interface engineering in optimizing spin injection processes.

Text: Electron transport through a quantum dot connected to three ferromagnetic leads under weak tunnel coupling conditions. A finite source-drain voltage induces a nonequilibrium spin polarization on the initially non-magnetic quantum dot, resulting in magnetoresistance. A ferromagnetic base electrode, which carries no current, modulates the quantum dot’s spin via incoherent spin-flip mechanisms and coherent spin precession. Since the dot’s spin state governs the device’s conductance, this interaction enables transistor-like operation controlled purely by magnetic means. We systematically examine how these spin dynamics influence the electric current across various geometric configurations, elucidating their distinct contributions to transport behavior. The findings highlight the interplay between spin coherence and decoherence in determining device functionality.

Text: We investigate the ground-state energies of a heavy-light K-Λ-like system as a function of interhadron distance \( r \), treating the heavy quarks as static. The resulting energies define an adiabatic potential \( V_a(r) \), which we employ to analyze the five-quark system's structure. Our lattice simulations utilize an anisotropic, asymmetric lattice with Wilson fermions, extracting energies from spectral densities via the maximum entropy method. The findings provide qualitative insights: solving the Schrödinger equation with \( V_a(r) \) yields bound-state wave functions suggesting the ground state resembles a hadronic molecule, while the first excited state, with a compact rms radius, aligns with a pentaquark-like five-quark cluster. We propose that all-light-quark pentaquarks may not exist, but heavy-quark pentaquarks could persist, though likely only in excited configurations.

Text: The generalized Pauli operators of a two-qubit system exhibit a notable geometric correspondence with the points of the generalized quadrangle \( W(2) \). Beyond the conventional associations with geometric hyperplanes, this framework reveals additional structures, including uni- and tri-centric triads and distinct pentads of Pauli operators. These findings highlight a deeper geometric interpretation of quantum operator sets, bridging combinatorial and algebraic properties of finite geometries with quantum information theory. The interplay between these mathematical constructs and quantum mechanics underscores the broader significance of finite projective spaces in quantum systems.

Text: The External Reality Hypothesis (ERH) posits an objective physical reality independent of human perception. When paired with an expansive definition of mathematics, this implies the Mathematical Universe Hypothesis (MUH), wherein the physical world is an abstract mathematical structure. This framework yields profound implications across physics, including symmetries, irreducible representations, units, free parameters, randomness, and initial conditions. Broader philosophical questions, such as consciousness, parallel universes, and Gödel incompleteness, also arise. I propose that only computable and decidable structures exist, resolving the cosmological measure problem and explaining the simplicity of physical laws. Additionally, the interplay between mathematical structures, computations, simulations, and physical systems suggests a deep, fundamental connection, reinforcing the MUH as a unifying paradigm.

Text: The dependence of leading pion-pion scattering amplitudes on the number of colors is examined using single-index and two-index quark fields. Text: The matrix-exponential closure offers a novel framework for turbulence modeling by reformulating traditional eddy-viscosity and nonlinear stress closures. This approach simplifies the stress transport equation by omitting pressure-strain correlations and other nonlinear terms, yet retains physical plausibility through assumptions of Lagrangian decorrelation and upstream isotropy. Its feasibility is demonstrated in Large Eddy Simulations (LES) of forced isotropic turbulence, where it provides a local, computationally efficient closure. Unlike conventional eddy-viscosity models, the matrix-exponential method derives directly from the stress transport equation, incorporating production terms while maintaining a structured, physically grounded formulation. This approach balances computational tractability with theoretical rigor, offering a promising alternative for turbulence closure in high-Reynolds-number flows.

Text: The dissipative infall of baryonic matter during galaxy formation significantly influences the structural evolution of dark matter halos. The findings demonstrate that the dissipative baryonic component repeatedly deflects orbits that frequently traverse the halo center. Additionally, the time-dependent gravitational potential arising from baryonic mass growth induces chaotic orbital behavior by shifting orbital frequencies, causing orbits to explore an expanded phase space. When this effect occurs on a substantial scale, it leads to a marked transformation in the halo's overall structure.

Text: This work examines the asymptotic behavior of large balls within an arbitrary locally compact group \( G \) exhibiting polynomial growth. Pansu’s foundational research, we establish that \( G \) is weakly commensurable to a simply connected solvable Lie group \( S \), termed the Lie shadow of \( G \). Furthermore, we demonstrate that large balls in \( G \) possess an asymptotic shape: upon appropriate renormalization, they converge to a compact limit set with geometric significance. The study also investigates the rate of convergence, explores illustrative examples, and presents an application to ergodic theory. Additionally, it resolves a query posed by Burago regarding left-invariant metrics and recovers prior results by Stoll on the irrationality of growth series in nilpotent groups.

Text: A spectroscopic approach for staggered fermions is introduced, leveraging thermodynamic principles. This method is validated through calculations of the Goldstone pion, with results for both dynamical and quenched systems aligning well with conventional spectroscopic techniques. While theoretically applicable to baryons and dibaryons, computational constraints render such analyses infeasible due to prohibitive resource demands. The approach thus offers a robust framework for meson spectroscopy but remains limited by practical computational barriers for heavier states.

Text: A comprehensive search was conducted for the near-infrared spectroscopic signature of the close-orbiting exoplanet HD 75289b. Given the phase-dependent separation of planetary and stellar spectra, a tomographic approach was employed to isolate potential absorption signatures. Due to the low signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) in individual exposures, a multiline deconvolution technique was applied to enhance the effective S/N by leveraging the ~100 expected planetary absorption lines within the 80 Å coverage. Text: This study examines the primary factors influencing the morphology of bilateral supernova remnants (BSNRs) observed in radio wavelengths, focusing on the roles of interstellar medium (ISM) density gradients and ambient magnetic field variations. Synchrotron radio emission is synthesized under varying assumptions regarding electron acceleration and injection. Results indicate that BSNR asymmetry arises when the line-of-sight is misaligned with either the density or magnetic field gradient. Additionally, we establish quantitative metrics to assess remnant asymmetry, offering insights into the microphysical processes governing strong shock waves.

Text: Recent research by Hansen demonstrated that the Wigner-Yanase entropy fails to exhibit subadditivity for general quantum states when decomposed into two subsystems, despite this property being valid for pure states. However, our findings indicate that this weaker subadditivity does not universally apply. We present a counterexample to illustrate this failure, thereby clarifying the limitations of the Wigner-Yanase entropy in multi-subsystem decompositions. This result underscores the necessity for caution when extending entropy properties to complex quantum systems.

Text: The cosmic microwave background (CMB) serves as a primordial relic of the Big Bang, encoding critical cosmological data. These techniques challenge the prevailing concordance model by scrutinizing the assumption that CMB temperature anisotropies conform to a statistically isotropic Gaussian random field on the sphere. Multiple studies have identified deviations from both statistical isotropy and Gaussianity, implying that alternative, more complex cosmological frameworks may be necessary. Additionally, spherical wavelet analyses have independently corroborated the existence of dark energy, reinforcing its role in cosmic dynamics. Text: Heavy-ion collisions involving neutron-rich nuclei offer a powerful method for studying the equation of state of isospin-asymmetric nuclear matter, particularly the density dependence of nuclear symmetry energy. This advancement has significant implications for nuclear effective interactions and the neutron skin thickness of heavy nuclei. Furthermore, theoretical progress in high-energy radioactive beam-induced reactions provides insights into the behavior of nuclear symmetry energy at higher densities. Text: Recent advancements by Hawking and Susskind posit that information remains conserved in the universe. This approach underpins the Dynamic Cores model, which examines information migration in such systems. The geometric flow on the statistical manifold is interpreted as a transition from localized dynamical systems in a d+1-dimensional AdS space to their delocalized holographic counterparts in a d-dimensional Conformal Field Theory (CFT). Notably, this flow aligns with renormalization group flow, facilitating the semi-conservation of informational invariants, which we interpret as distinct computational types.

Text: This study examines higher-derivative perturbations in quantum gravity and quantum field theories within curved spacetime, focusing on computational techniques for counterterms and short-distance expansions of Feynman diagrams. In more complex scenarios, contributions to the short-distance expansion are systematically classified, and various simplification strategies are proposed. The Campbell-Baker-Hausdorff formula is employed to circumvent limitations of traditional differential methods, particularly in higher-derivative contexts, thereby enhancing computational efficiency. This approach mitigates technical challenges while providing a structured framework for analyzing perturbative corrections in curved spacetime.

Text: A protocol for generating entanglement between spatially separated atoms within a single-mode optical cavity is presented. Notably, spatial variations in coupling constants facilitate transient entanglement, which persists over longer timescales than predicted for uniform coupling. When atoms are initially entangled, they may sustain entanglement indefinitely. Additionally, the entanglement exhibits a diffraction-like phenomenon, further enriching its dynamic properties. These findings highlight the nuanced interplay between atomic positioning, coupling strength, and entanglement persistence in cavity-based quantum systems.

Text: The Monte Carlo Model for Heavy-ion Therapy (MCHIT), developed using the GEANT4 toolkit (version 8.2), simulates nucleon and nuclear propagation in tissue-like media, incorporating projectile fragmentation and secondary nuclear interactions. The MCHIT accurately reproduces depth-dose distributions, fragment-specific dose contributions, positron-emitting fragment yields, and secondary neutron energy spectra at various angular positions. Additionally, radial dose profiles for primary nuclei and projectile fragments are computed, offering insights for biological dose assessment. Notably, near the Bragg peak periphery, secondary nuclear fragments contribute significantly to the transverse dose distribution. This work validates MCHIT as a reliable tool for heavy-ion therapy simulations, enhancing treatment planning and dosimetry accuracy.

Text: Consider the variety \( S \) of irreducible sextic curves with six cusps as their sole singularities. Let \( W \) represent an irreducible component of \( S \). The moduli space \( M_4 \) parametrizes smooth curves of genus 4. The moduli map from \( W \) to \( M_4 \) associates each general point of \( W \)—corresponding to a plane curve \( D \)—with the normalization of \( D \) in \( M_4 \). The number of moduli of \( W \) is defined as the dimension of the image of \( W \) under this map, which is known to be at most seven. This work demonstrates that both irreducible components of \( S \) achieve the maximal number of moduli, precisely seven.

Text: This study examines the interplay between superconducting pairing correlations and ferromagnetism in small metallic grains under a Zeeman field. Employing a universal Hamiltonian applicable in the large-Thouless-conductance limit, we demonstrate that an external Zeeman field can adjust the system into a coexistence regime where both superconducting and ferromagnetic correlations are present. By comparing exact solutions with mean-field theory, we find that the latter fails to accurately capture pairing correlations in intermediate regimes. Additionally, we analyze spin jumps at the phase boundary separating superconducting and coexistence phases, revealing distinct quantum transitions. These findings highlight the limitations of mean-field approximations and underscore the importance of exact methods in describing mesoscopic superconducting systems under magnetic fields.

Text: Phylogenetic mixture models are used to represent heterogeneous molecular evolution, a common phenomenon in biological data. Recent debates have focused on the performance of phylogenetic reconstruction methods when applied to data generated under such models. Critics argue that certain algorithms fail to recover the true tree topology, instead producing alternative topologies, and suggest this reflects methodological limitations. The implicit assumption underlying these critiques is that mixture-model-generated data on one topology can be reliably distinguished from data evolved on an unmixed tree of a different topology, given sufficient data and appropriate methods. However, we demonstrate that this assumption is not universally valid. Our findings indicate that combined data from two genes, whose phylogenetic trees differ only in branch lengths, can perfectly fit a tree of an entirely different topology. This challenges the notion that tree reconstruction methods can always distinguish between mixed and unmixed evolutionary scenarios.

Text: This study examines key aspects of nonabelian gauged linear sigma models (GLSMs), focusing on their role in describing partial flag manifolds—generalizations of Grassmannians—while emphasizing the physical realization of tangent bundles and other features relevant to (0,2) models. An analogous abelian GLSM exhibiting similar behavior is also reviewed. The study tentatively proposes a mathematical connection between these non-birational phases and Kuznetsov’s homological projective duality. Furthermore, it investigates the moduli spaces realized in these GLSMs, arguing for their physical significance.

Text: Wolfgang Pauli's exclusion principle, formulated over eight decades ago, has profound implications spanning femtoscopic to macroscopic scales, including relativistic and ultra-dense systems. This foundational concept underpins two key research areas in nuclear and astrophysics: hypernuclear physics and neutron star dynamics. The electromagnetic production of hypertritons and the role of neutrino electromagnetic properties in dense matter are particularly noteworthy. Additionally, the emerging field of econophysics presents a novel and intriguing domain for further exploration. These topics reflect the principle's enduring relevance and its capacity to bridge disparate areas of physical inquiry, from subatomic interactions to cosmic-scale phenomena.

Text: We analyze a population comprising an initial "Eve" lineage and neutral mutants, where the entire population eventually goes extinct. The dynamics of both the Eve-population and the total population are modeled using continuous-state branching processes, while neutral mutations are treated as an immigration process with intensity proportional to the population size. We derive a Williams' decomposition of the genealogy of the total population, represented as a continuous random tree, by conditioning on the ancestral lineage of the last surviving individual. This decomposition enables us to compute the joint extinction probability of the Eve-population and the total population in closed form. The framework provides a rigorous mathematical description of population dynamics under neutral mutation and extinction.

Text: Measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) represents a paradigm shift from traditional circuit-based models, emphasizing measurement as the core computational mechanism. Among MBQC frameworks, the one-way quantum computer is particularly notable for its foundational role. A key contribution is a calculus enabling local, compositional reasoning about these patterns. Additionally, a rewrite theory is introduced, accompanied by a standardization theorem that ensures any pattern can be transformed into a semantically equivalent standard form. This standardization facilitates a novel physical architecture where entanglement is pre-established, enhances parallelization by revealing measurement dependencies, and expands computational expressiveness. The results underscore MBQC's theoretical and practical significance.

Text: The refractive index of a dielectric medium, featuring both passive and inverted permittivity components, was evaluated through two distinct methodologies. The medium exhibited active properties, characterized by a negative real refractive index, thereby resolving a long-standing debate in the scientific literature. This dual-approach validation confirms the medium's anomalous refractive behavior, aligning with theoretical predictions of negative-index materials. Text: Recent studies on locally gated graphene layers forming p-n junctions have demonstrated quantum Hall effects in their electronic transport properties. This behavior arises from quantum Hall edge modes propagating along and across the p-n interface. In the bipolar regime, electron and hole modes interact at the junction, leading to current partitioning and quantized shot noise plateaus mirroring conductance quantization. Conversely, the unipolar regime exhibits noiseless transport. These phenomena highlight the massless Dirac nature of graphene’s charge carriers, with particle-hole symmetry playing a crucial role in mode mixing and noise generation in the bipolar state. The findings underscore the unique electronic properties of graphene under quantum Hall conditions.

Text: The Method of Invariant Grid (MIG) is an iterative approach for reducing chemical kinetic models by leveraging the concept of Slow Invariant Manifolds (SIMs). This work introduces a versatile numerical technique for constructing a discrete Quasi Equilibrium Manifold (QEM) in arbitrary dimensions, termed the Quasi Equilibrium Grid (QEG), alongside its associated algorithm. The QEG serves as a numerical framework for approximating the constrained minima of convex functions. The method's validity is demonstrated through the construction of one- and two-dimensional grids in model systems, showcasing its applicability in model reduction.

Text: Face recognition systems often suffer from degraded performance due to noise, corruption, and variations in input images. Experimental results demonstrate that the pairwise system outperforms the multiclass neural network in predictive accuracy when applied to face images corrupted by noise. The findings suggest that pairwise neural networks may offer a more robust solution for face recognition in challenging environments.

Text: This study examines the directed current dynamics in one-dimensional Dirac fermion systems subjected to local periodic potentials, considering both forward and backscattering interactions. Additionally, we analyze a confined system arranged in an annular geometry and coupled to a particle reservoir. A perturbative approach yields analytical expressions for the dc current in the lowest order of the potential amplitudes, supplemented by exact numerical solutions. These findings elucidate the interplay between periodic potentials and current behavior in low-dimensional Dirac fermion systems under non-equilibrium conditions.

Subsequently, the existence of Arakelov models of algebraic varieties over the rational numbers (ℚ) is demonstrated, with the developed methods applied to these models. This work provides a robust foundation for advancing Arakelov geometry within a broader algebraic context.

Text: A transmitter lacking channel state information (CSI) encodes a delay-limited Gaussian source using superimposed layers, where each layer progressively refines the preceding one. As signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) increases, additional power is allocated to lower layers, while higher layers retain their power allocation. Conversely, as the bandwidth ratio (channel uses per source symbol) approaches zero, the power distribution converges to that which maximizes expected capacity. The relationship between distortion and capacity remains a critical consideration in this framework.

Text: Photometric analysis of the deeply eclipsing SW Sex-type nova-like cataclysmic variable BH Lyn.
