The isospin chemical potential arises from a vacuum expectation value (vev) assigned to the temporal component of the non-Abelian gauge field on the brane. The fluctuations of this gauge field are dual to the SU(2) flavor current in the field theory. Focusing on the embedding where the quark mass vanishes, we compute all Green functions associated with the flavor current correlator. Our analysis reveals physical properties of these Green functions that extend beyond linear response theory, particularly demonstrating that the isospin chemical potential induces a frequency-dependent isospin diffusion coefficient. This result highlights the non-trivial dynamics of flavor currents in strongly coupled thermal plasmas.
Label: Mistral

Text: A continuous tensegrity structure, defined by a network of vertices, exhibits specific stress properties. If such a structure maintains strictly positive stress, it is bar equivalent, meaning its behavior can be replicated by a rigid framework of bars. Additionally, semipositive stress occurs precisely when the structure is partially bar equivalent. Minimally bar-equivalent tensegrities—those that lose bar equivalence upon removal of any subset of edges—necessarily possess strictly positive stress. Focusing on configurations where vertices form a rectifiable curve, we constrain motions to local isometries. Our analysis demonstrates that no local arclength-preserving deformation of a circle can increase any antipodal distance without simultaneously decreasing another, providing a rigorous proof of this geometric constraint.
Label: Mistral

Text: Cooperative diversity systems enhance wireless communication by leveraging user cooperation to counteract multipath fading, achieving diversity gains comparable to multiple-input single-output (MISO) channels. Under favorable conditions, these systems can match the outage probability of MISO systems across a broad signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) range. However, prior analyses often assume ideal synchronization, neglecting practical timing and frequency offsets. This study derives estimation bounds and maximum likelihood estimators for frequency offset estimation in cooperative networks, demonstrating that adaptive relay node frequency tuning reduces destination estimation errors. Additionally, an efficient estimation algorithm is proposed, utilizing data correlation sequences, which achieves mean squared error performance near the Cramer-Rao Bound, ensuring robust synchronization in cooperative communication systems.
Label: Mistral

Text: Numerical calculations employing the density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) method were used to analyze Friedel oscillations arising from the coupling of a quantum dot to one edge of a disordered one-dimensional wire in the Mott insulator regime. The study examined how disorder affects the decay rate of these oscillations, revealing that stronger electron-electron interactions mitigate the disorder's impact. This finding contrasts with prior observations in the Anderson insulator regime, where disorder was found to enhance the decay of Friedel oscillations. The results suggest that interaction strength plays a crucial role in modulating disorder effects, with Mott insulators exhibiting a distinct response compared to Anderson insulators. This interplay highlights the complex dependence of electronic correlations on disorder in low-dimensional systems.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines preference systems inherent to Peer-to-Peer (P2P) networks, which typically fall into three categories: global, symmetric, and complementary. A defining feature of these systems is their acyclicity, ensuring the existence of a stable (Pareto-efficient) configuration where no participant can improve their collaborations. The analysis demonstrates that any acyclic preference system can be represented by a symmetric mark matrix, facilitating the merging of such systems while preserving acyclicity. Additionally, the study explores structural properties of the corresponding collaboration graph, including clustering coefficient and diameter. Empirical analysis of latency-based preferences reveals that the stable configuration forms a small-world network, characterized by high clustering and short path lengths. These findings contribute to the understanding of P2P system dynamics and optimization.
Label: Mistral

Text: A cyclic cosmological model, wherein time neither commences nor ceases, is proposed as the most plausible framework. This hypothesis is elaborated in the concluding chapter, which may be read independently to grasp the foundational reasoning. However, engaging with the entire volume is recommended to fully appreciate the logical progression underlying the conclusions. The objective of this inaugural popular science work is to stimulate contemplation regarding the nature of time. Many non-specialists possess preexisting philosophical views on whether time has a definitive beginning or end. The book seeks to integrate recent scientific discoveries to refine and inform these reflections, addressing the two central questions posed in the title.
Label: Mistral

Text: Charge redistribution at interfaces significantly influences the electronic properties of high-Tc superconductors by altering their local band structure. This study investigates the normal-state electronic structure of YBa₂Cu₃O₇ (YBCO) at a YBCO-metal interface using first-principles calculations for representative interface configurations. The results quantify the intrinsic doping of the superconducting CuO₂ planes induced by the metal contact, attributed to a combination of band bending and local screening effects. The analysis reveals a net charge transfer of 0.09 to 0.13 electrons toward the intraplane Cu sites, with variations depending on the interface orientation. These findings provide insights into the electronic modifications at YBCO-metal junctions, which are critical for optimizing device performance in superconducting applications.
Label: Mistral

Text: The dynamic trafficking of α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazolepropionic acid receptors (AMPARs) between synaptic and extrasynaptic compartments is essential for synaptic plasticity. Prior research has examined endo/exocytotic mechanisms and the lateral diffusion of extrasynaptic receptors. Using single-molecule fluorescence microscopy, we directly visualized AMPAR movements in live neurons, revealing immobile and mobile receptors within synapses, exhibiting restricted diffusion, while extrasynaptic receptors displayed free diffusion. Receptors exchanged between these compartments via lateral diffusion. Glutamate application enhanced receptor mobility within synapses and increased the proportion of mobile receptors in juxtasynaptic regions. Blocking inhibitory transmission, thereby promoting excitatory activity, transiently elevated mobile receptor fractions while reducing juxtasynaptic receptor density. These findings demonstrate that synaptic plasticity is mediated by rapid, diffusion-regulated exchange of AMPARs between synaptic and extrasynaptic domains.
Label: Mistral

Text: The average transverse momentum of hadrons in high-energy collisions serves as a diagnostic tool for probing high-density (saturation) regimes in quantum chromodynamics. By introducing a transverse momentum cutoff and systematically varying its value, uncertainties in the leading-order hadron production cross section can be mitigated, allowing for a semi-quantitative exploration of distinct dynamical regions predicted by saturation physics. This approach provides a framework to analyze pion production at the Relativistic Heavy Ion Collider (RHIC) and the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). Quantitative predictions for the average transverse momentum of pions in kinematic regimes relevant to these experiments are derived, demonstrating the method's applicability to experimental data. The technique offers insights into the underlying saturation dynamics governing hadron production in high-energy collisions.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines the detailed spectroscopic abundances of 18 elements in 31 nearby planet-hosting stars. The derived abundances are integrated with data from analogous investigations of similar stellar populations and contrasted with a control sample of stars lacking detected planetary companions. The analysis reveals tentative evidence of abundance discrepancies between the two groups, particularly for aluminum, silicon, and titanium. These findings partially contradict a recent study based on the SPOCS database, highlighting the need for further investigation. The discrepancies underscore the importance of continued research into the chemical composition of planet-hosting stars to refine our understanding of stellar and planetary formation processes. Future studies should prioritize high-precision abundance measurements to resolve these inconsistencies and establish robust correlations between stellar chemistry and planetary systems.
Label: Mistral

Text: The white dwarf luminosity function has traditionally been determined using only the 1/V_max method, unlike other luminosity functions, which employ multiple techniques. However, this approach is susceptible to biases arising from statistical uncertainties or measurement errors. While prior research has examined statistical biases in various luminosity function estimators, this study focuses on biases introduced by observational inaccuracies and the contamination of input samples by distinct kinematic populations. To quantify these effects, a Monte Carlo simulation generates a synthetic population under controlled conditions. The analysis evaluates how different assumptions about measurement errors and sample contamination influence the derived disc white dwarf luminosity function, providing insights into the robustness and limitations of current methodologies.
Label: Mistral

Text: Recent multi-wavelength surveys, including optical, mid-infrared, and X-ray observations, have identified numerous obscured active galactic nuclei (AGN) candidates, particularly Type 2 quasars—powerful, heavily obscured counterparts to local Seyfert 2 galaxies. Despite the wealth of available multi-wavelength data, a comprehensive census and panchromatic characterization of the obscured AGN/quasar population remain elusive, primarily due to observational constraints. This review synthesizes recent advancements in obscured AGN identification, with an emphasis on Type 2 quasars selected from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey’s optical data. The findings highlight the challenges in obtaining a complete and unbiased sample, underscoring the need for improved observational strategies to fully characterize this enigmatic population.
Label: Mistral

Text: Optical tweezers were employed to investigate the mechanical properties of DNA molecules in the presence of intercalating drugs—daunomycin and ethidium bromide—under low-force conditions (0–2 pN). The persistence length of DNA-drug complexes exhibited a pronounced increase with rising drug concentration up to a critical threshold, beyond which it abruptly decreased and plateaued. Conversely, the contour length of the molecules increased monotonically, eventually reaching saturation. The critical drug concentrations at which the persistence length transition occurred aligned with previously reported values for the helix-coil transition of DNA-drug complexes, as determined by sedimentation experiments. These findings suggest a direct correlation between mechanical stiffness and structural transitions in DNA upon drug binding.
Label: Mistral

Text: Ionization fraction of the Barnard 68 pre-stellar core using millimeter-wavelength observations of H13CO+ and DCO+ lines, analyzed alongside a radiative transfer model incorporating grain depletion and gas-phase deuterium fractionation. When compared with prior CO and isotopologue observations, the data constrain metal abundances and the cosmic ionization rate. The H13CO+ emission aligns with metal abundances below 3×10⁻⁹ and a standard cosmic ray ionization rate, though complete metal depletion (cosmic rays as the sole ionization source) remains plausible at high visual extinctions. The DCO+ emission reveals sensitivity to the ortho-to-para H₂ ratio, suggesting a value of ~1×10⁻². The derived ionization fraction is approximately 5×10⁻⁹ relative to hydrogen nuclei. These findings refine our understanding of ionization processes in dense molecular clouds.
Label: Mistral

Text: Planetary topography can be analyzed using two primary models: as a load supported by the lithosphere or as a dynamic response to mantle convection-induced flexure. In both scenarios, the lithosphere's behavior under external forces is described by thin elastic plate or shell theory. On single-plate planets, the spherical geometry of the lithospheric shell significantly influences flexural processes. Previous studies have derived governing equations for spherical shell deformations and stresses, but only under the assumption of uniform thickness. However, geophysical observations indicate substantial lithospheric thickness variations. This work derives scalar flexure equations for a thin spherical shell with variable thickness or Young's modulus. The resulting equations are solvable sequentially, except for a coupled system where solutions yield transverse deflection and another parameter.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study applies a quantum field theoretical renormalization group framework to investigate vortex dynamics in magnetically coupled layered superconductors, extending prior work on Josephson-coupled systems. A two-dimensional multi-layer sine-Gordon model is formulated, representing the system as a gas of topological excitations. By carefully selecting the mass matrix, the model captures vortex-dominated phenomena in magnetically coupled superconductors. The analysis yields the established interaction potentials of fractional flux vortices, while also identifying key physical parameters such as vortex fugacity and temperature. The phase structure of the multi-layer sine-Gordon model is examined using differential renormalization group techniques, revealing a transition temperature dependence on layer number consistent with prior theoretical and experimental findings. This approach provides a rigorous, first-principles derivation of critical behavior in magnetically coupled superconductors.
Label: Mistral

Text: Galactic X-ray binaries, characterized by rapid variability, serve as ideal systems for studying accretion physics and associated phenomena, particularly outflows. By comparing jet behavior in black hole and neutron star X-ray binaries, this work examines the role of jets at extremely low Eddington ratios. Preliminary findings are presented from fitting the broadband spectral energy distribution of a quiescent black hole binary using a "maximally jet-dominated" model. This approach provides insights into the dominance of jets in low-luminosity states, contributing to our understanding of accretion processes in compact objects. The analysis highlights the significance of jets in regulating energy output and spectral properties under such conditions, advancing our knowledge of accretion physics across different regimes.
Label: Mistral

Text: Electrostatic interactions between oppositely charged planar surfaces are analyzed as functions of salt concentration and surface charge densities (which need not be equal). An analytical criterion for the transition between attractive and repulsive forces is derived, bridging the high-salt regime (exponentially screened interactions, as described by Parsegian and Gingell) and the zero-salt limit (governed by inter-plate separation and Gouy-Chapman length, per Lau and Pincus). At low salt and high surface charges, the attractive pressure exhibits long-range behavior, independent of the charge asymmetry. This attraction arises from counter-ion depletion as the plates approach. The theory also predicts multiple distinct interaction regimes, depending on experimental parameters. The results unify prior models and extend their applicability.
Label: Mistral

Text: We precisely compute the von Neumann and topological entropies of the toric code as functions of system size and temperature, focusing on regimes where the energy gap between magnetic and electric excitations is infinitely large. In this limit, magnetic loop structures remain intact, while thermal fluctuations disrupt electric loop configurations. Our analysis reveals that entanglement entropy exhibits singular dependence on both temperature and system size, with non-commuting zero-temperature and infinite-size limits. The topological entropy, derived from entanglement, abruptly halves at any infinitesimal temperature in the thermodynamic limit and stabilizes at this reduced value as temperature increases. Finite systems, however, exhibit smooth, continuous decay. For finite but large energy scale separations, we assert that these findings remain valid.
Label: Mistral

Text: ECHO is a novel numerical code designed for high-precision simulations of three-dimensional, time-dependent general relativistic magnetohydrodynamics (GRMHD) and magnetodynamics (GRMD). It employs an Eulerian Conservative High Order scheme, enabling shock-capturing capabilities with arbitrary formal accuracy for smooth flows, surpassing existing GRMHD/GRMD methods limited to second-order precision. The framework operates within the 3+1 Eulerian formalism, accommodating diverse equation sets, algorithms, and generic spacetime metrics, facilitating seamless integration with solvers for Einstein’s equations. High-order reconstruction techniques and a two-wave approximate Riemann solver enhance its robustness. The induction equation is handled via Upwind Constrained Transport (UCT) to maintain the divergence-free condition of the magnetic field. Additionally, ECHO supports the magnetodynamic limit, equivalent to force-free degenerate electrodynamics.
Label: Mistral

Text: We conducted self-consistent 2.5-dimensional nonsteady magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulations to study jet formation over extended durations, incorporating accretion disk dynamics. Prior nonsteady MHD simulations of astrophysical jets demonstrated that their characteristics closely resemble those of steady jets, but these studies were limited by short computational timescales relative to observed jet lifetimes. Our work examines long-term evolutions of mass accretion and outflow rates, jet velocity, and energy fluxes, revealing quasi-periodic jet ejection. The periodicity correlates with the time required for the initial magnetic field to twist and generate a toroidal component. Our findings are compared with steady-state theoretical models and earlier 2.5-dimensional nonsteady MHD simulations, providing insights into the sustained dynamics of astrophysical jets.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines the gauge/gravity duality for a finite-temperature field theory with a finite isospin chemical potential, utilizing a setup of two coincident D7-branes embedded in an AdS-Schwarzschild black hole background. The isospin chemical potential arises from a vacuum expectation value (vev) assigned to the temporal component of the non-Abelian gauge field on the brane. Fluctuations of this gauge field correspond to the SU(2) flavor current in the dual field theory. Focusing on the embedding where the quark mass vanishes, we compute all Green functions associated with the flavor current correlator. Beyond linear response theory, these Green functions reveal notable physical properties, including a frequency-dependent isospin diffusion coefficient induced by the isospin chemical potential. The analysis provides deeper insights into the dynamics of flavor currents in strongly coupled field theories at finite density.
Label: Mistral

Text: The substitutional Cr³⁺ ion in MgAl₂O₄ spinel was analyzed using Cr K-edge EXAFS and XANES spectroscopies, supplemented by first-principles calculations that closely matched experimental data. The Cr-O bond length aligns with that in MgCr₂O₄, suggesting full relaxation of the first coordination shell, while the second shell of Al atoms exhibits partial relaxation. These findings indicate deviation from Vegard’s law in the MgAl₂O₄-MgCr₂O₄ solid solution. Despite local angular distortions, the D₃d symmetry of the octahedral B-site is preserved upon Cr substitution. Structural relaxation occurs via strain-induced bond buckling, involving angular tilts of Mg-centered tetrahedra around the Cr-centered octahedron. Notably, the angles between edge-sharing octahedra, which form aligned chains, remain largely unchanged. This study elucidates the local structural adjustments accommodating Cr incorporation in the spinel lattice.
Label: Mistral

Text: The incorporation of a fifth large-scale space-time dimension into orbital dynamics, as previously examined, successfully explained galactic rotation curves and the Pioneer Anomaly without altering Newtonian mechanics, merely reformulating them within a five-dimensional context. This framework introduced an acceleration parameter, \(a_r\), integral to the revised equations and yielded a proposed value for \(a_r\). This companion study extends the analysis by reformulating the conservation of energy within the same five-dimensional framework, offering an independent method to estimate \(a_r\) using fundamental cosmological constants, including the gravitational constant \(G\). The findings suggest that the inverse-square law of gravitation may arise directly from energy conservation principles in this expanded dimensional framework. This approach provides a novel, dynamics-independent derivation of \(a_r\) and reinforces the theoretical consistency of the five-dimensional model.
Label: Mistral

Text: Non-local field equations, rather than conventional local formulations such as the Dirac or Klein-Gordon equations, may be employed to model scattering processes. Derived from relativistic energy principles and the correspondence principle, these equations incorporate square root operators. When coupled to an electromagnetic field and expanded to quadratic order in the coupling constant \( e \), they enable the computation of scattering matrix elements within quantum electrodynamics, including Compton scattering and identical-particle interactions. This analysis focuses on the scalar case, comparing results with those obtained via the Klein-Gordon equation. Additionally, a framework for extending these insights to spin-1/2 systems is outlined. The approach demonstrates an alternative formalism for describing relativistic quantum phenomena while maintaining consistency with established theoretical predictions.
Label: Mistral

Text: In turbulent flows, the motion of small particles with relaxation times comparable to the flow's inertial scales exhibits inertial drift due to their finite inertia. This drift velocity closely aligns with the Lagrangian velocity increments of turbulence at the particle's relaxation time. When considering two nearby particles, their collective drift induces rapid fluctuations in local velocity increments, necessitating a Langevin framework to model their separation dynamics. This approach facilitates the analysis of the Lyapunov exponent and yields a Richardson-like law governing particle separation beyond the viscous scale. The interplay between particle inertia and turbulent fluctuations thus governs their relative dispersion, providing insights into chaotic mixing and particle transport in turbulent environments.
Label: Mistral

Text: Young open clusters are potential sites for cosmic ray acceleration, supported by TeV gamma-ray detections near Cyg OB2 and Westerlund 2, as well as their association with unidentified EGRET sources. Several mechanisms for particle acceleration within these clusters have been proposed, including shocks in massive stellar winds, pulsars and their nebulae, supernova remnants, and massive compact binaries. This study examines the radiation processes involving both accelerated electrons and hadrons within open clusters, focusing specifically on particle acceleration at shocks generated by Wolf-Rayet (WR) star winds. Particles diffuse through the cluster’s medium during the WR star’s active phase, interacting with ambient matter and radiation fields. Initially, these interactions occur within the cluster, followed by subsequent encounters with denser surrounding clouds. The study assesses the resulting high-energy emissions from these processes.
Label: Mistral

Text: Advanced electromagnetic potentials, intrinsic to Maxwell’s classical theory, are often dismissed as undesirable, leading to the artificial suppression of time-symmetry. This exclusion is unnecessary and, in some cases, detrimental. We examine the direct-action theory, where advanced and retarded potentials coexist symmetrically, precluding the addition of arbitrary complementary functions to the wave equation’s solution. A key challenge is explaining the observed asymmetry, typically attributed to the Wheeler-Feynman absorber theory, which fails due to incompatible cosmological boundary conditions. We propose that cosmological redshift in expanding universes inherently satisfies the required boundary condition, aligning the cosmological and thermodynamic arrows of time. Consequently, the direct-action formulation of electromagnetism emerges as the preferred theoretical framework.
Label: Mistral

Text: A hierarchical simulation framework has been devised to assess the dependability of a high-availability commercial RAID storage system incorporating a cache architecture. This approach employs three abstraction layers to model the cache structure, operational processes, and fault-tolerance mechanisms. Each layer evaluates the effects of faults and errors within the cache and disk subsystems. Corresponding simulation submodels, developed using the DEPEND environment, facilitate fault injection, error detection, and recovery simulation, enabling quantitative dependability assessments. The framework incorporates multiple fault models to simulate component failures, disk malfunctions, transmission errors, and data corruption in both cache memory and storage media. This structured methodology allows for comprehensive analysis of system resilience under diverse fault conditions.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines the relic density of non-relativistic, long-lived or stable particles (χ) in non-standard cosmological frameworks. We compute the relic abundance from arbitrary initial temperatures in the radiation-dominated era, establishing a lower bound on the initial temperature (T₀ ≥ mχ/23) under the assumption that thermally produced χ particles constitute the observed dark matter density. This constraint applies universally, regardless of the χ annihilation cross section. Additionally, we explore scenarios with altered expansion rates, demonstrating that an approximate formula akin to the standard case accurately predicts the final relic abundance. By selecting the χ annihilation cross section to match the observed cold dark matter density in standard cosmology, we impose constraints on potential modifications to the expansion rate at temperatures around T ∼ mχ/20, preceding Big Bang nucleosynthesis.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines the potential to distinguish between Modified Newtonian Dynamics (MOND) and Newtonian gravity with dark matter by analyzing the vertical kinematics of disk galaxies. We compare models with identical equatorial circular velocities—purely baryonic disks in MOND and the same disks in Newtonian gravity embedded in spherical dark matter halos—by solving the Jeans equations under a two-integral distribution function. Results indicate that deep-MOND disks exhibit significantly higher vertical velocity dispersions than their Newtonian counterparts. However, this distinction diminishes in high-surface-density disks, suggesting that current observational data may lack the sensitivity to reliably discriminate between MOND and Newtonian gravity with dark matter. Thus, further refinements or additional observational constraints are necessary to resolve this ambiguity.
Label: Mistral

Text: Gauge-mediated supersymmetry (SUSY) breaking framework incorporating a D-term mechanism. R-symmetry breaking, essential for generating gaugino masses in the Standard Model, arises from gaugino condensation within a strongly coupled hidden-sector gauge theory. The energy scale of this strong dynamics must align with the messenger mass scale \( M \); otherwise, perturbative calculations would yield negative squared soft masses for squarks and sleptons, invalidating the model. Consequently, the mass spectrum is predominantly governed by the parameter \( \sqrt{D}/M \). The model accommodates a broad gravitino mass range, from approximately 1 eV to 100 TeV. Additionally, potential realizations of this framework within string theory are explored, highlighting its theoretical versatility.
Label: Mistral

Text: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) exhibit unique properties that make them highly suitable for field emission applications. However, experimental observations reveal significant variability in their field emission performance. To design efficient field emission devices, a systematic, multiphysics modeling approach is essential to analyze these variabilities. This study presents a computational model of randomly oriented CNTs within a thin film, integrating field emission phenomena, electron-phonon transport, and the mechanical behavior of individual CNTs. The model employs a time-incremental scheme to update the CNT states dynamically. Device current is calculated using the Fowler-Nordheim equation, enabling performance assessment at the macroscopic scale. This framework provides a comprehensive tool for optimizing CNT-based field emission devices by accounting for their inherent variability.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines the thermodynamic properties of a fuzzy sphere within the framework of matrix quantum mechanics, specifically the BFSS model augmented by a Chern-Simons term. Perturbative calculations are performed to all orders, leveraging the one-loop dominance of the effective action in the large-N limit. The fuzzy sphere exhibits instability at sufficiently strong coupling, with the critical point precisely determined as a function of temperature. Monte Carlo simulations map the complete phase diagram, revealing exact agreement with perturbative results above the critical point. Below this threshold, where perturbation theory fails, a Hagedorn transition is observed. In the high-temperature regime, the model reduces to a totally reduced system, clarifying its connection to established findings. The analysis integrates perturbative and non-perturbative methods to elucidate the phase structure and critical behavior of the system.
Label: Mistral

Text: We conducted a spectroscopic search for the near-infrared signature of the close-orbiting exoplanet HD 75289b. Using the Phoenix spectrograph at Gemini South, we acquired approximately 230 spectra spanning 2.18–2.19 microns. By analyzing the combined star-planet light, we sought the absorption profile predicted by irradiated atmospheric models. Since the planetary signal is phase-dependent, we employed orbital modeling and tomographic techniques to isolate potential absorption features. Due to the low signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) in individual exposures, we applied multiline deconvolution to enhance the S/N by averaging ~100 planetary lines within the 80-angstrom wavelength range. Despite achieving a mean line S/N of ~800 post-deconvolution, no detectable absorption signature was identified. This null result suggests either an insufficient signal or limitations in current observational or modeling techniques. Further refinements may be necessary to constrain the planet’s atmospheric properties.
Label: Mistral

Text: A comprehensive search for wide, faint companions within the 5-million-year-old Lambda Orionis open cluster was conducted using optical, near-infrared, and Spitzer/IRAC photometry. The study identified a highly unusual wide binary system, LOri167, comprising a brown dwarf and a planetary-mass candidate separated by 5 arcseconds (2000 AU). Effective temperatures of 2125 K and 1750 K were determined for the two components. If confirmed as cluster members, theoretical models suggest masses of 17 ± 3 M_Jup and 8 ± 5 M_Jup, respectively. The extreme separation of this binary challenges conventional formation models, particularly those proposing substellar objects arise from circumstellar disks around higher-mass stars. Such wide, low-mass binaries remain rare and pose significant constraints on current substellar formation theories.
Label: Mistral

Text: Modern ground-based observatories, such as the VLT’s VISIR instrument, achieve diffraction-limited mid-infrared imaging (≈0.3 arcsec), resolving the flared structures of protoplanetary disks around Herbig Ae stars. While radiative transfer models have successfully replicated the spectral energy distributions (SEDs) of these stars, the spatial constraints provided by high-resolution imaging remain underutilized. This study evaluates whether existing hydrostatic disk models—designed to fit SEDs—can also reproduce observed mid-infrared images. VISIR observations at 8.6 and 11.3 µm reveal a prominent flared disk around HD97048, inclined relative to the line of sight. To analyze these spatial data, we employ a disk model incorporating key physical parameters, including stellar irradiation, disk geometry, and dust properties, to assess consistency between model predictions and observed disk morphology.
Label: Mistral

Text: A central unresolved problem in liaison theory concerns whether every homogeneous Cohen-Macaulay ideal in a polynomial ring is *glicci*—that is, whether it belongs to the G-liaison class of a complete intersection. This study resolves the question affirmatively for Stanley-Reisner ideals associated with weakly vertex-decomposable simplicial complexes, a category encompassing matroid, shifted, and Gorenstein complexes. Additionally, a counterexample is provided demonstrating that the *glicci* property is not invariant across different characteristics of the base field. The findings also contribute novel support for two of Stanley’s conjectures: one regarding partitionable complexes and the other concerning Stanley decompositions. The results leverage advanced liaison-theoretic techniques to advance these longstanding conjectures in combinatorial commutative algebra.
Label: Mistral

Text: The linewidths observed in interstellar neutral hydrogen (HI) emission spectra exhibit distinct families, with characteristic widths of approximately 34, 13, and 6 km/s. These values correlate with the critical ionization velocities (CIVs) of the most abundant interstellar atomic species. Notably, the 34 km/s linewidth, potentially linked to helium’s CIV, is ubiquitous, appearing in both low-velocity local HI profiles and high-velocity clouds. Similar linewidths have been documented in intergalactic structures such as the Magellanic Stream, Very-High-Velocity Clouds, and Compact High-Velocity Clouds. If CIV effects influence interstellar dynamics, they may generate localized electron density enhancements where fast-moving neutral gas interacts with ambient plasma. Supporting evidence suggests this phenomenon occurs, though further investigation is required to confirm its prevalence and mechanisms.
Label: Mistral

Text: In a quantum wire with a weak confining potential and low electron density, electrons may arrange into a two-row (zigzag) Wigner crystal. The spin interactions in this system are governed by nearest-neighbor and next-nearest-neighbor exchange, as well as three- and four-particle ring exchange processes. The resulting spin chain exhibits a diverse phase diagram, featuring fully polarized and partially polarized phases alongside unpolarized states. These include antiferromagnetic order, dimerized configurations, and a distinct phase induced by four-particle ring exchange. The interplay of these exchange mechanisms leads to a rich variety of magnetic behaviors, dependent on electron density and interaction strength. This system provides a model for studying correlated electron phases in reduced dimensions.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study introduces a novel computational framework for modeling the Fe Kα line profiles emitted by geometrically thick, sub-Keplerian accretion disks orbiting rotating black holes. As accretion rates rise, thin Keplerian disks transition to thicker, sub-Keplerian configurations, necessitating a fully relativistic treatment to accurately capture the impact of disk geometry and orbital dynamics on spectral line shapes. The developed code represents the first such computation, enabling precise simulations of relativistic effects while also facilitating the generation of synthetic disk images. This approach provides critical insights into how disk thickness and deviations from Keplerian rotation influence observed line profiles, advancing our understanding of accretion physics in strong gravitational fields.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study theoretically examines adiabatic quantum pumping in a normal conductor interfacing the normal regions of two superconducting Josephson junctions. By modulating the superconducting phase difference as the pumping parameter, we demonstrate the generation of a finite pumped charge. The mechanism relies on the dynamic evolution of superconducting phases via the ac Josephson effect, enabling operation at high frequencies and yielding pumped currents on the order of nanoamperes. The theoretical framework is supported by an analysis of experimental feasibility, highlighting potential applications in high-frequency quantum electronic devices. The results underscore the interplay between phase coherence and nonequilibrium transport in hybrid superconducting-normal metal systems.
Label: Mistral

Text: Observational data suggests the presence of massive planets orbiting at distances exceeding several hundred astronomical units (AU) from their host stars, where core accretion timescales exceed the typical disc lifetime. Such planets may have formed near their stars and subsequently migrated outward. We investigate how angular momentum transfer via viscous disc interactions—mediated by a massive inner planet—could drive significant outward migration of a smaller outer planet. Our findings indicate that outward migration to large orbital radii is theoretically feasible. However, several factors, including disc dissipation, gravitational perturbations, and non-linear dynamical effects, may complicate this process, potentially limiting its efficiency or feasibility under certain conditions. Further research is required to fully constrain these mechanisms.
Label: Mistral

Text: Cold denaturation arises from the temperature-dependent behavior of hydrophobic interactions, as demonstrated by constant-pressure simulations of a hydrophobic model in explicit solvent. The hydrophobic effect, which stabilizes molecular associations at moderate temperatures, weakens at low temperatures, favoring solvent-separated configurations over direct hydrophobic contacts. This destabilization mirrors the mechanism observed in pressure-induced denaturation, where hydrophobic interactions are disrupted. The findings suggest that cold denaturation in real proteins may similarly result from the temperature-sensitive nature of hydrophobic interactions, leading to the preferential solvation of hydrophobic regions over their mutual association. This mechanism provides a unified explanation for denaturation driven by both low temperatures and high pressures.
Label: Mistral

Text: The analysis of neutrino flavor composition from astrophysical sources offers insights into both emission mechanisms and fundamental neutrino properties. However, achieving these dual objectives is challenging, as precise knowledge of the source neutrino fluxes is required to disentangle emission processes from propagation effects. This study critically evaluates the expected flavor ratios and energy spectra across various astrophysical sources, highlighting theoretical uncertainties arising from incomplete understanding of their internal structures. The interplay between emission models and neutrino oscillations complicates the extraction of definitive conclusions, necessitating further observational and theoretical refinements to advance our comprehension of neutrino physics and astrophysical environments.
Label: Mistral

Text: Falsifiable predictions serve as critical validations for scientific models, yet nonlinear multiparameter models often exhibit sloppiness, where parameter sensitivities vary widely across multiple orders of magnitude. This property complicates the extraction of precise predictions, as small changes in certain parameter combinations can yield disproportionate effects. Sloppiness also undermines the reliability of linear uncertainty approximations, which may fail to capture nonlinear behaviors, and introduces computational challenges in Monte Carlo-based uncertainty quantification. Furthermore, it influences the types of experimental data most effective for constraining model predictions. To address these issues, we propose a standardized test problem and advocate for improved model communication practices, emphasizing transparency in parameter sensitivity and uncertainty propagation. These refinements would enhance model robustness and interpretability.
Label: Mistral

Text: Near the supermassive black hole at the Galactic center (SgrA*), observations reveal several elongated filaments and two cometary-shaped dusty structures. Their morphology and kinematics do not align with purely Keplerian orbital dynamics expected in the black hole’s gravitational potential. This discrepancy suggests alternative mechanisms govern their formation and motion. The filaments’ properties likely stem from an outflow originating in the circumstellar disks of young, mass-losing stars orbiting SgrA*. Additionally, the black hole itself may contribute to this outflow. Supporting evidence and theoretical models indicate the outflow may be collimated, possibly due to magnetic or hydrodynamic processes. These findings highlight the complex interplay between stellar winds, accretion dynamics, and black hole feedback in the Galactic center environment.
Label: Mistral

Text: The Lorentz transformations, when applied to the spacetime coordinates of a single event, yield differential forms that clarify the conditions for time dilation, length contraction, and relativistic simultaneity. We quantify the simultaneity error inherent in relativity theory and trace these effects to a finite phase velocity within the moving reference frame. This phase velocity is analogous to the de Broglie wave associated with a moving particle. Extending this concept to stationary particles, we demonstrate that the phase of de Broglie waves—and similarly, the phase of electromagnetic waves transporting energy at light speed—remains a relativistic invariant, consistent across all observers. Consequently, the universe and its matter-energy content may be interpreted as a superposition of propagating waves, unified by their invariant phase relationships.
Label: Mistral

Text: The interaction between a two-level atom in a double-well potential and a single cavity mode of an electromagnetic field modifies the atom's tunneling dynamics. The coupling between internal atomic states and external motion, mediated by photon emission into the cavity, induces quasiperiodic tunneling behavior. Under specific parameter regimes, this coupling leads to a collapse and revival of tunneling, a phenomenon characterized by periodic suppression and restoration of tunneling probability. By employing a laser to probe and manipulate the atom's internal degrees of freedom, precise control over its spatial localization—such as confinement to a single well—can be achieved. These effects are experimentally accessible using atoms in optical double-well traps, providing a platform to study and exploit cavity-mediated tunneling dynamics.
Label: Mistral

Text: A comprehensive examination of experimental findings on Ultra-High Energy Cosmic Rays (UHECRs) reveals a broad consensus regarding spectral features, including the Second Knee, the Ankle, and the GZK Cutoff. However, the composition of the primary particle flux remains contested, complicating the interpretation of these spectral characteristics. Additionally, while initial observations suggested potential anisotropies in arrival directions, subsequent independent studies have failed to confirm these findings, leaving the origin of UHECRs largely unresolved. The absence of conclusive evidence regarding primary composition and source identification underscores the need for further experimental and theoretical investigation to elucidate the underlying mechanisms governing UHECR propagation and acceleration.
Label: Mistral

Text: NGC 5466 exhibits a blue straggler population that is less centrally concentrated than the horizontal branch (HB) or red giant branch (RGB) stars. No increase in blue straggler frequency is observed at greater distances from the cluster's core. Dynamical friction models suggest that, if the cluster's current density profile has persisted over time, blue stragglers should exhibit stronger central concentration. Notably, NGC 5466 possesses an unusually low central density relative to similarly luminous clusters. Despite this, its blue straggler specific frequency aligns precisely with the established correlation between straggler frequency and cluster absolute magnitude (M_V) seen in other systems. This suggests that NGC 5466's blue straggler population adheres to broader cluster trends, despite its atypical structural properties.
Label: Mistral

Text: Recent discussions have synthesized 11 years of exoplanet discoveries via radial velocity, transit, and microlensing methods, yielding over 200 confirmed detections, excluding pulsar-hosted systems. A planet is defined as a self-gravitating, spheroidal body exceeding 10²⁶ grams, originating from residual protostellar disk material. Protostellar radiation drives disk expansion, facilitating planet formation through radial agglomeration. Outer planets emerge gradually via classical dynamical processes in the snow zone, while dense cores result from accretion. However, no detected exoplanets meet this definition. Instead, they are cryptoplanets—formed from protostellar ejecta. During high-latitude accretion, protostars expel angular momentum via equatorial outflows, trapping ejected material in disk-like structures. These cryptoplanets, though planet-like, originate from distinct formation pathways, challenging traditional definitions.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study proposes a quantum-mechanical framework for modeling conceptual representation, wherein concepts are encoded as vectors within a complex Hilbert space. Membership weights of items are derived via quantum probabilities, adhering to quantum mechanical principles. The theory is applied to conceptual disjunction, demonstrating that empirical membership weights align with quantum predictions, particularly when accounting for interference, superposition, and contextual effects. Deviations from classical logic—manifesting as overextension or underextension—are attributed to these quantum phenomena. A graphical interpretation elucidates how these effects arise within the quantum model, highlighting the role of interference and contextual modulation in shaping conceptual disjunction. The findings underscore the utility of quantum formalism in capturing nuanced cognitive processes beyond classical frameworks.
Label: Mistral

Text: ASTROD I is a proposed single-spacecraft mission designed to employ interferometric and pulse-ranging techniques to achieve high-precision measurements of solar system parameters and enhance tests of relativistic gravity. Central to the spacecraft is a test mass, which the vehicle will track via a drag-free control system to preserve geodesic motion. However, cosmic rays and solar particles can induce electrostatic charging of the test mass, disrupting its trajectory. Using the GEANT4 simulation toolkit and a simplified geometric model, we estimated that the test mass will accumulate a positive charge at a rate of 24 ± 7 e⁺/s during solar minimum, primarily due to cosmic ray protons and alpha particles (³He and ⁴He). These findings allow us to quantify the resulting disturbances on the test mass's motion, ensuring accurate mission performance.
Label: Mistral

Text: Since 2003, the refurbished 24-inch Heyde-Zeiss telescope at Konkoly Observatory, Budapest, has been used to observe approximately a dozen field RR Lyrae stars. The primary focus has been the Blazhko modulation, a poorly understood phenomenon characterized by periodic variations in the light curves of these stars. The resulting multicolour CCD observations are unprecedented in scope and precision, enabling the detection of subtle amplitude modulations. Notably, the discovery of Blazhko stars with low modulation amplitudes suggests that the occurrence of this phenomenon is more widespread than previously believed. This underscores the urgency of investigating its underlying mechanisms. A summary of the observational data and findings from the past three years is provided.
Label: Mistral

Text: Characteristic subsets of a group remain unchanged under all automorphisms. Focusing on fundamental groups of closed hyperbolic surfaces, we examine word length relative to characteristic generating sets formed by finite unions of automorphism orbits. Our analysis demonstrates that the translation length of any element with non-zero crossing number is strictly positive and bounded below by a constant. This lower bound depends solely on an upper limit of the crossing numbers of the generators, a result that explicitly resolves a query posed by B. Farb. The findings underscore the interplay between group automorphisms, geometric properties, and combinatorial length metrics in hyperbolic surface groups.
Label: Mistral

Text: Melanin is a diverse group of biological pigments ubiquitous in living organisms, categorized into three primary types: eumelanins in humans, pheomelanins in animals, and allomelanins in plants. Eumelanin, a biopolymer, is of particular scientific interest due to its semiconductor-like properties and its role in human cellular photoprotection. However, its precise structural configuration remains incompletely understood. The pigment predominantly consists of planar 5,6-indolequinone molecules and their reduced derivatives, semiquinone and hydroquinone. This study investigates the structural, electronic, and optical properties of eumelanin monomers and dimers in various charge states (neutral and anionic) under vacuum conditions. Semiempirical computational methods—MNDO, AM1, PM3, and ZINDO/S-CI—were employed to analyze the neutral charge state, providing insights into its molecular behavior.
Label: Mistral

Text: A weak MgII absorption system was identified at redshift z~0.452 in the high-resolution UVES spectrum of the quasar HE0001-2340. The weaker of the two MgII components exhibited associated neutral species (SiI, CaI, and FeI), a novel observation in quasar spectra. To determine the absorber's nature, its properties were compared with those of weak MgII systems, damped Ly-α systems, and local interstellar clouds, while photoionization models were used to reproduce its ionization conditions. Although the system's equivalent width classifies it as a weak MgII absorber, its relative transition strengths diverge significantly from typical absorbers. The estimated probability of intersecting such a system along a quasar sightline is approximately 0.03. The detection of rare neutral transitions suggests substantial neutral hydrogen shielding within the cloud.
Label: Mistral

Text: Grid of PHOENIX spectra for red giant and supergiant stars, covering optical and near-infrared wavelengths. Notably, it introduces models with adjusted surface abundances of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, addressing modifications induced by convective dredge-up and rotational mixing. The primary objectives are to evaluate the accuracy of current models in replicating observed spectra, assess the spectral impact of altered abundances, and determine their influence on derived stellar parameters. At solar metallicity, observed giant star spectra align well with models down to approximately 3400K. Modified abundances are favored in a minority of luminosity class II cases, suggesting potential excess mixing beyond standard first dredge-up. Supergiant stars exhibit greater near-infrared spectral diversity, with successful fits achieved for roughly one-third of the sample.
Label: Mistral

Text: Particle sizes involved in powder snow avalanches by analyzing the processes particles undergo before incorporation into the avalanche body. The research focuses on the boundary layer at the avalanche base, where experimental data reveal the size range of particles susceptible to entrainment. The study further assesses whether these particles can ascend to the boundary layer's upper region, thereby contributing to the avalanche. Findings indicate that the most prevalent suspended particles in such events are smaller than 200 micrometers. This size threshold is critical for understanding avalanche dynamics and particle transport mechanisms. The results provide empirical insights into the physical constraints governing particle entrainment and suspension in powder snow avalanches.
Label: Mistral

Text: Performance of signaling strategies characterized by high peak-to-average power ratios in both coherent and noncoherent fading channels. The analysis focuses on two recently developed modulation techniques: on-off binary phase-shift keying (OOBPSK) and on-off quaternary phase-shift keying (OOQPSK). For these schemes, the optimal detection criteria are derived, and closed-form expressions for error probabilities are obtained. Numerical methods are utilized to compute these probabilities under varying conditions. The findings demonstrate that increasing signal peakedness enhances energy efficiency by reducing error rates at fixed power levels, suggesting a trade-off between peak power and system performance. The results provide insights into the design of energy-efficient communication systems in fading environments.
Label: Mistral

Text: In scenarios where sensors or encoders face Byzantine attacks—where an unknown subset of sensors is compromised by an adversary to disrupt data reconstruction at a fusion center—three distinct problem formulations arise. The first involves a variable-rate framework, where the decoder dynamically selects transmission rates for each sensor. The minimal achievable sum rate in this context is explicitly defined as the maximum entropy across all distributions indistinguishable from the true source distribution by the decoder. Additionally, two fixed-rate variants are examined: one employing deterministic coding and another utilizing randomized coding. While both fixed-rate approaches yield achievable rate regions, randomized coding achieves a broader region, though neither matches the efficiency of variable-rate coding. These findings highlight the inherent trade-offs between adaptability and optimality in distributed source coding under adversarial conditions.
Label: Mistral

Text: The Super-Kamiokande (SK) analysis of neutrino events, including fully and partially contained interactions, assumes that the zenith angle of the incident neutrino matches that of the detected charged lepton. This study evaluates the validity of this assumption by examining muon-like events resulting from quasi-elastic scattering (QEL). To do so, a Time Sequential Monte Carlo Simulation was developed, rigorously modeling all relevant physical processes without approximations. By comparing the zenith angle distributions of emitted muons under the SK assumption with those derived from the Time Sequential Simulation, the analysis concludes that the SK method accurately measures the direction of incident neutrinos for events occurring within the detector. This validation supports the reliability of SK’s directional reconstruction for contained neutrino interactions.
Label: Mistral

Text: Low-temperature expansions (LTEs) provide a framework to express the nucleation free energy and precipitate interface free energy as functions of the solubility limit in alloys that precipitate stoichiometric L1₂ compounds, such as Al-Sc or Al-Zr alloys. By applying classical nucleation theory, a simplified expression for the nucleation rate is derived, validated through comparisons with atomic-scale simulations. LTEs further elucidate why conventional mean-field approximations, including the Bragg-Williams model, inaccurately predict nucleation rates in ordering alloys. The discrepancies arise from the inability of mean-field methods to capture the intricate energetics and ordering effects that LTEs incorporate. This analysis underscores the necessity of advanced theoretical approaches to accurately model precipitation kinetics in such systems.
Label: Mistral

Text: Computational framework for mitigating inter-state conflict through a neural network-driven feedback control system. By simultaneously regulating four key parameters—Democracy, Dependency, Allies, and Capability—the model successfully prevented all predicted conflict outcomes. Notably, controlling either Dependency or Capability alone also eliminated all anticipated disputes, suggesting these variables exert disproportionate influence. An analysis of individual input variables confirmed that Dependency, Capability, and Democracy are the primary determinants of conflict likelihood. These findings highlight the potential for targeted policy interventions to stabilize international relations by systematically adjusting these critical factors. The results underscore the efficacy of a data-driven, multi-variable approach in conflict prevention.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines self-organization mechanisms in a two-level solid-state class-B laser system, focusing on the roles of a nonlinear absorber and an external force. Under separate influences, self-organization emerges via Hopf bifurcation, yielding stable pulsed radiation. Stability is assessed through Floquet exponent analysis. The nonlinear absorber expands the parameter domain sustaining stable periodic emission, while an external force inhibits self-organization. When both factors interact, the system exhibits complex behavior, including two reentrant Hopf bifurcations, complicating the self-organization dynamics. These findings elucidate the interplay between nonlinear absorption and external forcing in laser systems, highlighting their competing effects on stability and periodicity.
Label: Mistral

Text: Supernova 1987A in the Large Magellanic Cloud offers an unprecedented opportunity to study the aftermath of a core-collapse supernova. Initially, the ejected material expanded at velocities exceeding 10% of light speed but decelerated upon encountering dense circumstellar material shed by the progenitor star. This interaction generated a shock wave, producing brightening radio synchrotron emission, which the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA) resolved into an expanding, limb-brightened shell. Over 15 years of ATCA observations at 0.4 arcsecond resolution revealed an accelerating expansion rate, increasing from approximately 3600 km/s in 1992 to 5200 km/s by late 2006. While X-ray measurements of the expanding shell consistently yielded diameters ~15% smaller than radio observations, a combined Fourier analysis of both datasets reconciled this discrepancy, confirming consistent expansion dynamics across wavelengths.
Label: Mistral

Text: The evaluation of high-risk scenarios at road intersections has emerged as a critical focus in automotive research. This study presents an innovative methodology for computing risk functions by integrating a highly nonlinear processing model with an advanced information encoding technique. Static and dynamic elements within intersection scenes are encoded using directed, positional, acyclic labeled graphs. The risk assessment problem is then reframed as an inductive learning task, executed by a recursive neural network (RNN). RNNs, as connectionist models, are adept at handling supervised and unsupervised learning tasks structured as directed acyclic graphs. The efficacy of this approach is validated through predefined scenarios. Unlike existing methods, this framework uniquely learns the structural representation of risk, enhanced by the synergy between a robust encoding scheme and a nonlinear processing model. This dual-layered approach offers a more nuanced and adaptive risk assessment framework.
Label: Mistral

Text:
We derive a rational Lax matrix of size \(R+1\) from its spectral curve, obtained via desingularization of the characteristic polynomial, along with supplementary data. Employing a twisted Cauchy-like kernel—a bi-differential of bi-weight \((1-
u, 
u)\)—we establish a residue formula for the Lax matrix entries, expressed in terms of dual differential bases of weights \(
u\) and \(1-
u\). All constructs are explicitly defined using Theta functions. Through a series of "elementary twists," we generate sequences of Lax matrices sharing the same spectral curve and polar structure, interconnected via conjugations by rational matrices. Specific twist selections yield sequences of Lax matrices associated with finite-band recurrence relations (difference operators) of identical form. Such recurrences underpin various orthogonal and biorthogonal polynomial systems, with the derived formulas offering insights into their large-degree asymptotic behavior.
Label: Mistral

Text: By examining the parallels between socioeconomic systems, modeled via evolutionary game theory, and physical systems described by quantum mechanics, we demonstrate that these seemingly distinct frameworks exhibit fundamental equivalences. Quantum mechanics' extensions into statistical physics and information theory provide conceptual tools to elucidate behaviors in economics and biology. The replicator dynamics in evolutionary games correspond to the von Neumann equation in quantum systems. A system where all agents achieve Nash equilibrium mirrors a maximum entropy state in statistical mechanics. Nature can be conceptualized as a game where players—whether organisms, firms, or individuals—compete for collective welfare, adhering to equilibria that prioritize systemic stability over individual gain. This alignment suggests that cooperative dynamics, driven by collective benefit, underpin both biological and socioeconomic equilibria.
Label: Mistral

Text: The self-force exerted on a scalar-charged particle following a generic geodesic in the Schwarzschild spacetime is computed by evaluating the retarded scalar field generated by the particle's motion. This field is numerically determined using a fourth-order convergent finite-difference scheme in the time domain. Due to the singular nature of the retarded field along the particle's worldline, a regularization procedure is necessary. The mode-sum regularization method, originally developed by Barack and Ori, is employed to address this singularity. The paper details the numerical methodology, validation tests, and results for both mildly eccentric and "zoom-whirl" orbits, demonstrating the robustness of the approach.
Label: Mistral

Text: The age distribution of stars with planetary companions reveals distinct trends. While most known planetary systems orbit stars younger than 5 billion years, those with planets within 0.4 AU exhibit a more uniform age distribution, suggesting long-term dynamical stability. Some older, lower-metallicity host stars display elevated [α/Fe] ratios, indicative of thick-disk membership. If terrestrial planet frequency correlates with stellar metallicity, the median age of such systems is estimated at approximately 3 billion years. These findings have significant implications for the search for Earth-like exoplanets, particularly among nearby stars, where older, stable systems may offer prime targets for habitability studies. The interplay between stellar age, metallicity, and planetary architecture thus informs strategies for identifying Earth analogues.
Label: Mistral

Text: Polycrystalline electroceramic materials exhibit complex charge transport mechanisms influenced by multiple contributions, including electrode-sample interfaces, grain boundaries, and bulk effects. To accurately assess their functionality, these components must be deconvoluted. Temperature-dependent alternating current impedance spectroscopy was employed to investigate spinel manganate-based thermistor ceramics, revealing three distinct relaxation phenomena. Each displayed thermally activated resistivity, with grain boundary and interface contributions exhibiting markedly different capacitances, facilitating clear differentiation. The study underscored the composite nature of dielectric properties in polycrystalline ceramics and demonstrated impedance spectroscopy as an effective tool for modeling and analyzing such behavior. This approach provides critical insights into the electrical and dielectric characteristics of functional electroceramic materials.
Label: Mistral

Text: Numerical magnetohydrostatic solutions characterizing the equilibrium of cool, dense prominence plasma within a gravitationally stratified, near-potential coronal magnetic field. The solutions, derived using the FINESSE solver, elucidate the magnetic field configurations supporting prominence structures and their associated plasma. The equilibrium conditions vary across subdomains, with free boundaries requiring continuity or jump conditions to ensure force balance. A finite element elliptic solver enables the computation of solutions previously unattainable through analytical methods, incorporating temperature and entropy as free functions of the magnetic flux, along with diverse polytropic indices, cross-field temperature gradients, and photospheric shear near polarity inversion lines. Notably, one solution accurately replicates the classic three-part prominence structure, demonstrating the model's capability to capture observed phenomena.
Label: Mistral

Text: The Pseudo-Spectral Spatial Domain (PSSD) technique is employed to investigate carrier wave shocking, with a focus on diagnostic verification against theoretical predictions for dispersionless conditions. The study highlights sensitivity to Carrier Envelope Phase (CEP) and pulse bandwidth in the single-cycle regime. PSSD's adaptable dispersion management permits independent control of linear and nonlinear dispersion, facilitating analysis of carrier self-steepening and shock formation. Results reveal distinct asymmetries between normal and anomalous dispersion, influencing both the shock regime boundaries and near-shocked pulse waveforms. These findings provide experimental insights into achieving carrier shocking or extreme self-steepening, offering practical guidance for future implementations.
Label: Mistral

Text: This study examines algebraic commutation relations in the Lagrangian quantum field theory of free scalar, spinor, and vector fields from a mathematical perspective. The Heisenberg equations for dynamical variables and a uniqueness condition for operator representations under specific Lagrangians serve as the foundation for these relations. Paracommutation relations, or their generalizations, emerge as the most general framework satisfying all Heisenberg equations. However, the simultaneous fulfillment of these equations and the uniqueness requirement proves impossible. This conflict is resolved by redefining dynamical variables through a procedure analogous to normal ordering, which modifies permissible commutation relations. The introduction of the vacuum state further restricts the admissible relations, reducing the redefinition to normal ordering. These constraints collectively narrow the viable commutation relations in quantum field theory.
Label: Mistral

Text: An analysis of bibliometric indicators across the 100 largest European universities reveals a size-dependent cumulative advantage in citation impact, consistent with prior findings at the research group level, suggesting this phenomenon is systemic in academic performance. Lower-performing institutions exhibit a more pronounced advantage in citation accumulation with increasing size compared to top-tier universities. Additionally, larger lower-performing universities demonstrate a significant reduction in the proportion of uncited publications, while higher journal impact correlates inversely with uncited output. Notably, top-performing universities maintain high research performance across diverse activities, indicating that scale does not dilute average output. These findings imply that institutional size and strategic publication practices collectively influence citation dynamics and overall research impact.
Label: Mistral

Text: Arabic speech recognition by proposing an innovative framework for an Arabic Automated Speech Recognition (ASR) system. The approach leverages the open-source CMU Sphinx-4 platform, developed by Carnegie Mellon University, which employs discrete Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) for large-vocabulary, speaker-independent, continuous speech recognition. By utilizing Sphinx-4’s utilities, we construct a model tailored to Arabic phonetics and linguistic structures. The research evaluates the system’s adaptability to Arabic, assessing its performance in recognizing continuous speech across diverse speakers. This work contributes to advancing Arabic ASR technology, demonstrating the feasibility of repurposing existing frameworks for underrepresented languages. The findings highlight potential improvements and challenges in deploying robust, language-specific speech recognition systems.
Label: Mistral

Text: The theoretical investigation examines charge current generation driven by spin dynamics in systems exhibiting the Rashba spin-orbit interaction. Under conditions of electron disorder, exchange coupling, and spin-orbit interactions are analyzed perturbatively. The primary charge current arises from the conversion of spin current via the inverse spin Hall effect, with an additional contribution from a fictitious conservative field. These findings are applied to the scenario of a moving domain wall, demonstrating the interplay between spin dynamics and charge transport in spin-orbit-coupled systems. The results highlight the role of spin-orbit interactions in mediating charge current generation through spin-dependent mechanisms.
Label: Mistral

Text: Linear polarization in X- and gamma-ray emissions provides critical insights into the geometry, magnetic fields, and radiation processes of astrophysical sources. Despite its diagnostic potential, few X-ray polarization measurements exist, and these are limited to mono-energetic detections. Many sources, however, are expected to exhibit energy-dependent polarization signatures. This study explores the feasibility of detecting such variations using the Compton scattering technique, specifically with the proposed PoGOLite balloon experiment, which operates in the 25-100 keV energy range. Geant4 simulations were conducted using a PoGOLite model and input spectra derived from Cygnus X-1 and accreting magnetic pulsars (100 mCrab). Simulations were performed for effective observing times of 6 and 35 hours, corresponding to standard and extended flight durations. Both smooth and abrupt energy-dependent polarization variations were analyzed and compared to constant polarization scenarios using chi-square statistics. The results indicate that constant polarization across energy can be rejected for the Cygnus X-1 spectrum in its hard state.
Label: Mistral

Text: The IEEE 802.11e standard enhances the Medium Access Control (MAC) layer of the original IEEE 802.11 framework to facilitate Quality-of-Service (QoS) in Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs). Its Enhanced Distributed Channel Access (EDCA) mechanism introduces multiple Access Categories (ACs), each with distinct Contention Window (CW) sizes, Arbitration Interframe Space (AIFS) values, and Transmit Opportunity (TXOP) limits to enable MAC-level QoS differentiation. This study presents an analytical model of EDCA that precisely accounts for CW, AIFS, and TXOP variations under diverse traffic conditions, while also assessing the impact of MAC layer buffer size on performance. Both analytical and simulation results validate the model’s accuracy across varying traffic loads, EDCA parameter configurations, and buffer capacities, confirming its robustness in performance evaluation.
Label: Mistral

Text: Concurrent challenges of detecting and identifying an abrupt, unobservable shift in the probability distribution function (pdf) of a sequence of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables, where the post-change pdf belongs to a finite set of alternatives. The primary goals are rapid detection of the change and precise estimation of the resulting pdf. Employing a Bayesian framework, a novel sequential decision-making strategy is derived and mathematically proven to be optimal. The geometric characteristics of this strategy are illustrated through numerical simulations, providing empirical validation of its theoretical properties. The approach ensures efficient change-point detection while minimizing inference errors, offering a robust solution for dynamic probabilistic environments.
Label: Mistral

Text: The mass spectra of both heavy and light mesons are derived using the relativistic flux tube model, achieving strong agreement with experimental data when flux tube contributions—including retardation and spin-orbit effects—are augmented by a one-gluon-exchange potential, quark self-energy corrections, and instanton-induced interactions. The model requires no arbitrary constants to determine the absolute mass scale, as parameters are constrained by lattice QCD calculations, minimizing theoretical arbitrariness. The approach is further validated by comparing the computed glueball spectrum with lattice QCD results. Additionally, the model’s predictions are linked to the nature of recently observed experimental states, such as the f₀(1810), X(3940), and Y(3940), providing insights into their underlying structure.
Label: Mistral

Text: Phase closure at 682 GHz and 691 GHz was pioneered using three antennas of the Submillimeter Array (SMA) on Mauna Kea, Hawaii. On September 19, 2002, phase closure was first demonstrated at 682.5 GHz with an artificial ground-based beacon. Three days later, astronomical detections of Saturn and Uranus were achieved at 691.473 GHz, corresponding to the CO(6-5) transition, across all three baselines (25.2 m, 25.2 m, and 16.4 m). Despite Saturn’s heavy resolution even on these short baselines, phase closure was successfully obtained for Uranus and Callisto. This marked the first phase closure in this frequency range. Additionally, the CO(6-5) line was detected in Orion BN/KL and other Galactic sources, alongside the 658 GHz vibrationally excited water maser line in evolved stars. These observations also yielded the first arcsecond-scale images in this band, representing a significant milestone in submillimeter interferometry.
Label: Mistral

Text: Transcriptional regulation is often conceptualized as a binary switch, where gene expression is modulated by transcription factor (TF) concentration. However, recent analyses of gene expression noise suggest that regulatory systems can transmit far more than a single bit of information. Achieving this optimal capacity requires precise coordination among three key parameters: the dynamic range of TF concentrations, the input-output function of the regulatory module, and the noise levels associated with TF binding and transcription. These constraints must satisfy specific matching conditions. Experimental data from the Bicoid/Hunchback system in early *Drosophila* embryos support this framework, demonstrating that the system operates near its theoretical information transmission limit, reaching approximately 90% of the maximum possible capacity. This agreement underscores the predictive power of the model and highlights the efficiency of biological regulatory networks.
Label: Mistral

Text: Role of network analysis in English language acquisition, proposing criteria for annotating child speech corpora. Theoretically, it argues that syntactic networks reflect the application of syntactic mechanisms rather than their intrinsic properties. Observable network patterns provide insights into the grammar's functional organization and capacity, though not its underlying structure directly. Methodologically, the paper addresses challenges in modeling early-child language, which lacks well-defined syntactic structure. To mitigate this, it introduces systematic annotation criteria grounded in constituency hierarchy and lexico-thematic relations, enabling the construction of syntactic networks from projected dependencies. This approach facilitates the analysis of emergent linguistic patterns in child language development.
Label: Mistral

Text: The KArlsruhe TRItium Neutrino (KATRIN) experiment seeks to measure the absolute neutrino mass scale with unprecedented precision, targeting a sensitivity of 0.2 eV while minimizing model-dependent uncertainties compared to cosmological and neutrinoless double-beta decay constraints. This is achieved by analyzing the integral electron energy spectrum near the endpoint of tritium beta decay, where the neutrino mass and endpoint energy are jointly determined as free parameters. Right-handed currents, which modify the electron spectrum near the endpoint, introduce systematic effects in neutrino mass extraction. Statistical analyses indicate that unaccounted right-handed couplings, constrained by prior beta decay experiments, can bias the fitted neutrino mass by up to 5–10% when the endpoint is treated as a free parameter. However, fixing the endpoint as an input parameter—an incorrect approach—can amplify this bias significantly, potentially altering the inferred neutrino mass by an order of magnitude.
Label: Mistral

Text: Field theories traditionally rely on closed exterior forms, which encode conservation laws governing physical fields. This paper establishes that such forms arise from equations describing conservation (balance) laws for material systems. The derivation process reveals a fundamental link between field-theoretic equations and those governing material media, indicating that the theoretical foundations of field theories are inherently constrained by the conservation laws of material systems. This relationship underscores the necessity of grounding field theories in the underlying principles of material dynamics, thereby unifying the mathematical frameworks of both disciplines. The analysis highlights the interdependence of these domains, suggesting that advancements in one area may inform and refine the other.
Label: Mistral

Text: Ground-state magneto-optical resonance in cesium vapor confined within an Extremely Thin Cell (ETC), where the cell thickness matches the wavelength of the incident light.
